title: Chrome Ad Blocker with 10M+ Installs Found with Dormant Script Injection Capability
blurb: Researchers at Island found that Adblock for YouTube, a Featured Chrome extension with over 10 million installs, contains dormant infrastructure to run arbitrary JavaScript on any site via a single server-side config change, with no extension update or store review. No malicious payload has been distributed, but the capability has existed since February 2025 and ties the extension to related ad blockers already pulled for malware.

title: DeepReinforce releases Ornith-1.0 open-source coding models
blurb: DeepReinforce open-sourced Ornith-1.0, a family of agentic coding models spanning a 9B Dense edge build up to a 397B MoE flagship, all trained on Gemma 4 and Qwen 3.5 foundations. The models learn to write their own task-specific scaffolds alongside solutions during RL, and DeepReinforce reports the 397B version hitting 77.5 on Terminal-Bench 2.1 and 82.4 on SWE-Bench Verified, matching Claude Opus 4.7.

title: STOCKSTAY Another Day: The Latest Addition to Turla’s Intelligence Gathering Apparatus
blurb: Google's threat intel team detailed STOCKSTAY, a .NET backdoor Russia-linked Turla has deployed against Ukrainian government and military targets since 2022.

title: New Gaslight macOS Malware Uses Prompt Injection to Disrupt AI-Assisted Analysis
blurb: Researchers documented Gaslight, a Rust-based macOS implant that embeds a prompt injection payload designed to make an AI-assisted triage agent doubt its own session and abort analysis of the artifact. SentinelOne attributes the tool to North Korea-aligned actors with high confidence and notes it attacks the analyst's LLM perception rather than the sandbox, alongside a Telegram-based C2 channel and a Python infostealer.

title: How we built saga rollbacks for Cloudflare Workflows
blurb: Cloudflare shipped saga rollbacks for Workflows, letting developers declare compensation logic inside each step.do() call to reverse completed steps when a later step fails. The pattern handles cases like cross-bank transfers, where a committed debit cannot be undone directly and must be reversed through a new idempotent operation, keeping rollbacks as durable as the forward path.

title: Anthropic accuses Alibaba of campaign to 'brazenly' and 'illicitly' extract AI capabilities
blurb: Anthropic accused Alibaba of the "largest known distillation attack" against its models, telling the Senate Banking Committee that Alibaba-affiliated operators ran 28.8 million exchanges through roughly 25,000 fraudulent accounts between April 22 and June 5. The letter follows earlier Anthropic claims against DeepSeek, Moonshot, and MiniMax, and lands as the company juggles a Trump administration export directive restricting foreign access to its latest Claude models.

title: Zig's new bitCast semantics and LLVM back end improvements
blurb: Zig redefined @bitCast semantics and reworked its LLVM backend to lower arbitrary-width integers as ABI-sized types, fixing missed optimizations and miscompilations.

title: Scaling AI Inference Across Multiple GPUs Using NVIDIA TensorRT with Multi-Device Inference Support
blurb: TensorRT 11.0 adds multi-device inference support, bringing native multi-GPU execution to the TensorRT runtime while preserving kernel fusions, memory planning, and quantization. The feature integrates NCCL for transport across NVLink, NVSwitch, PCIe, and InfiniBand, and supports tensor and context parallelism so PyTorch models too large for a single GPU can deploy out-of-framework via Torch-TensorRT.

title: Qualcomm acquires AI startup Modular in open ecosystem bet to challenge CUDA
blurb: Qualcomm acquired Modular, the AI infrastructure startup founded by LLVM and Swift creator Chris Lattner, to build an open software layer that challenges Nvidia's CUDA lock-in. Modular's stack lets developers port the same model across Nvidia, AMD, Arm, and Apple hardware, giving Qualcomm a "write once, run anywhere" compute layer as it pushes into data center AI. Terms were undisclosed; Modular last raised at a $1.6B valuation.

title: Kraken in talks to buy 15% stake in DeFi lender Aave at $385 million valuation
blurb: Kraken is in talks to buy a 15% stake in DeFi lending protocol Aave at a $385 million valuation, investing 35,000 ETH (around $71 million) for 250,000 AAVE tokens plus common equity. The deal is the first in a planned series for Payward Asset Management, signaling Kraken's parent pushing deeper into DeFi ahead of a potential IPO, even as Aave rebuilds from April's KelpDAO exploit that triggered billions in withdrawals.

title: European Commission lines up Amazon and Microsoft for cloud gatekeeper status
blurb: The European Commission is preparing to designate Amazon and Microsoft as cloud "gatekeepers" under the Digital Markets Act, which would subject AWS and Azure to stricter rules on interoperability and customer lock-in. The label, already applied to firms like Apple and Google, could force changes to egress fees and data portability across Europe's two largest cloud providers.

title: Cellebrite said it cut off Russia, but Russia used its tools anyway
blurb: Citizen Lab researchers found a Russian investigative unit used Cellebrite's phone-hacking tool to break into the iPhone of jailed opposition politician Andrey Pivovarov in June 2021, three months after Cellebrite announced it would immediately stop selling to Russian government agencies. The case underscores that once forensic and surveillance tools reach a customer, vendors often can't claw them back, and license revocation does little to prevent continued abuse.

title: Qualcomm reveals HBC near-memory AI architecture, AI250 and AI350 accelerators — touts 6x higher bandwidth-per-watt compared to HBM, 200x capacity compared to on-chip SRAM
blurb: Qualcomm introduced HBC, a near-memory compute architecture that places its AI accelerator directly beneath an LPDDR DRAM stack connected by through-silicon vias, alongside the AI250 and AI350 data center accelerators. The design skips HBM and its costly silicon interposer, with Qualcomm claiming 6x higher bandwidth-per-watt than HBM and over 200x the capacity of on-chip SRAM, though it has not disclosed absolute bandwidth figures.

title: Deno 2.9
blurb: Deno 2.9 ships deno desktop, an experimental command that compiles a script or web framework project into a native, self-contained desktop app where the UI runs in a webview and logic runs in Deno, no Electron or Tauri toolchain required. The release also lets deno install read npm, pnpm, yarn, and Bun lockfiles directly, and adds CSS module imports, a stronger test runner, faster startup, and Node.js 26 compatibility.

title: Russia uses Cellebrite to break into human rights activist’s phone, even after cancellation of contract
blurb: Russian authorities used Cellebrite's UFED tool to crack the phone of imprisoned activist Andrey Pivovarov around June 2021, months after the company canceled its Russian contracts, according to a Citizen Lab report. The case shows that legacy forensic hardware with offline modes keeps working long after vendors cut ties, and investigators say data from the phone may have helped the FSB target other dissidents.

title: Anthropic claims that China's Alibaba used 25,000 fake accounts and 28.8 million exchanges to illicitly 'distill' its Claude model — violations occurred from April to June 2026
blurb: Anthropic told the U.S. Senate Banking Committee that Alibaba used 25,000 fake accounts and 28.8 million Claude exchanges between April and June 2026 to illicitly distill its models. The lab tied the campaign to operators linked to Alibaba and its Qwen AI unit, following earlier accusations that DeepSeek, Moonshot, and MiniMax ran 24,000 fraudulent accounts to train on Claude's output.

title: Dolphin Emulator Progress Release 2606
blurb: The Dolphin team published Progress Report 2606, the latest roundup of changes to the open-source GameCube and Wii emulator since the previous release. The report walks through accuracy fixes, performance work, and compatibility improvements contributed by volunteers, continuing Dolphin's long-running cadence of documenting each batch of merged changes for its userbase.

title: Coinbase-incubated Base suffers mainnet chain stall interrupting block production
blurb: Base, the Coinbase-incubated Ethereum layer-2 and most active scaling solution by transaction volume, suffered a major mainnet outage that interrupted reliable block production after a problematic block stalled subsequent block building. Deposits, withdrawals, and transactions were delayed or stalled, and the failure landed the same day as Base's scheduled Beryl upgrade, though it appeared separate from the planned maintenance.

title: Anthropic accuses Alibaba of using 25,000 fake accounts to scrape Claude AI
blurb: Anthropic accused Alibaba of running nearly 25,000 fraudulent accounts to extract capabilities from its Claude models, generating over 28.8 million exchanges between April 22 and June 5 in what it called the largest such attack against it. The company said the campaign used distillation to replicate Claude's abilities at lower cost, echoing earlier alleged efforts by DeepSeek, Moonshot AI, and MiniMax amid rising US-China scrutiny of model extraction.

title: Apple says "the rapid expansion of AI data centers has created an extraordinary surge in demand for memory and storage"; the MacBook Neo rises from $599 to $699 (Mark Gurman/Bloomberg)
blurb: Apple warned that the AI data-center buildout has driven an "extraordinary surge in demand for memory and storage," pushing component costs up across its lineup. The pressure has already forced a price hike on the entry-level MacBook Neo, which rises from $599 to $699, signaling that the memory shortage squeezing data centers is now reaching consumer hardware.

title: Apple Raises Prices on Some Products by $500
blurb: Apple is raising prices on Macs, iPads, home devices, and the Vision Pro by up to $500 to offset a memory and storage chip shortage. Prices for the iPhone, Apple Watch, and AirPods stay flat, suggesting Apple is absorbing the component squeeze on its highest-volume products while passing costs to lower-volume lines.

title: EU Held Talks With White House on Anthropic After Mythos Cutoff
blurb: The EU held talks with the Trump administration over access to Anthropic's most advanced AI models after the US restricted foreign nationals from using the technology earlier this month under the Mythos cutoff. The discussions underscore how export controls on frontier models have become a diplomatic flashpoint, with allied governments now negotiating for access that was previously open.

title: OpenAI unveils first chip as part of Broadcom deal in effort to 'build the full stack'
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom introduced Jalapeño, OpenAI's first custom AI chip, designed for inference and manufactured by Broadcom. The launch advances OpenAI's push to build its full stack and reduce reliance on Nvidia GPUs, following recent compute deals with AWS, AMD, and Cerebras as President Greg Brockman said the company "cannot get compute fast enough.

title: The engineer who built Swift and LLVM sold his AI startup to Qualcomm for $4B
blurb: Qualcomm is acquiring Modular, the AI infrastructure startup co-founded by LLVM and Swift creator Chris Lattner, in an all-stock deal worth about $3.92B. The hardware-agnostic platform lets developers write AI applications once and run them across Nvidia, AMD, and Apple chips without CUDA, giving Qualcomm a vendor-neutral software stack already used by Oracle, Amazon, Nvidia, and AMD at double Modular's $1.6B valuation from nine months ago.

title: IBM stacks up a sub-nanometer chip future
blurb: IBM demonstrated a transistor process node it claims can scale down to 1 Angstrom, roughly a tenth of a nanometer, extending Moore's Law-style density gains past today's leading-edge limits. The work relies on stacked nanosheet and complementary FET architectures, signaling a path to denser, more efficient chips even as conventional planar scaling runs out of room.

title: Apple to Skip High-End M6 Mac Chips in Favor of AI-Focused M7 Line
blurb: Apple will skip the high-end M6 generation for Macs and jump straight to an AI-focused M7 line, launching M7 Pro, M7 Max, and M7 Ultra chips as its next top-end processors. The move marks one of the biggest shifts in Apple's Mac silicon strategy, prioritizing on-device AI performance over its usual incremental generational cadence.

title: Sources: Google expands the scope of its months-old AI coding strike team to "midtraining" to try to catch up with Anthropic, after major executive departures (Erin Woo/The Information)
blurb: Google expanded its months-old AI coding strike team to cover "midtraining" as it works to close the gap with Anthropic on code-generation quality. The push follows major executive departures and signals that Google sees the intermediate training stage, between pretraining and fine-tuning, as the lever for matching Claude's lead in developer tools.

title: Adobe Acquires AI Upscaling Specialists Topaz Labs
blurb: Adobe acquired Topaz Labs, the Dallas-based maker of Emmy-winning AI upscaling, denoising, and sharpening tools like Gigapixel and Topaz Photo. Adobe will integrate Topaz's enhancement models across Photoshop, Lightroom, and Firefly to compete with Canva and DaVinci Resolve, while keeping the standalone Topaz apps running under CEO Eric Yang. The deal is expected to close in the second half of 2026.

title: Qualcomm plans China-specific data center chips — new Dragonfly lineup will include nerfed AI accelerators that comply with export thresholds
blurb: Qualcomm will bring all four of its Dragonfly data center product lines to China, including AI accelerators engineered to stay below US export thresholds, CEO Cristiano Amon told Nikkei Asia. The first chip, the AI250, ships next year using an HBC near-memory design instead of the tight-supply HBM stacks Nvidia and AMD rely on, as Qualcomm targets a market where Beijing is steering buyers toward Huawei and Cambricon.

title: AI and Liability
blurb: A German court ruled that Google is liable for its AI search summaries, rejecting the defense that users should know not to trust AI-generated information blindly. The decision tests a decades-old carrier-versus-publisher distinction: unlike traditional search that quotes and links third-party content, AI overviews rewrite other people's words, exercising the kind of editorial discretion that has historically made publishers legally responsible for what they produce.

title: Unconventional AI releases its first model, built on an oscillator architecture its founder says could cut power use by a factor of a thousand
blurb: Unconventional AI released Un-0, an image generation model that runs on a simulated oscillator architecture and matches diffusion models like Stable Diffusion, according to its research paper. Founder Naveen Rao says the coupled-oscillator approach, which abandons digital logic for analog physics, could cut AI power use 1000x, though the chip does not yet exist and the figure is a theoretical projection.

title: Which tokens does a hybrid model predict better?
blurb: Olmo Hybrid and Olmo 3, two 7B models matched on data, tokenizer, and training recipe, were compared token-by-token to isolate what hybrid architecture actually changes. The hybrid wins on meaning-bearing tokens like nouns, verbs, and pronoun referents that require tracking context, while transformers hold their edge on tokens copied verbatim from earlier in the input, where attention can look up the exact answer.

title: Apple Hikes Mac, iPad Prices on Memory Shortage; Shares Fall
blurb: Apple raised prices across its Mac, iPad, home device, and Vision Pro lines to offset an unprecedented memory chip shortage driving up component costs. The across-the-board hike, rare for Apple outside of new product launches, sent shares lower as investors weighed the hit to demand and the squeeze that DRAM and storage scarcity is putting on hardware margins.

title: OpenAI gets spicy with Broadcom via AI data movement-optimized ‘Jalapeño’ chip
blurb: OpenAI unveiled "Jalapeño," its first custom AI chip, co-designed with Broadcom to cut data movement and balance compute, memory, and networking for inference workloads. The accelerator went from design to tape-out in nine months and marks the first step in a multi-generation roadmap that reduces OpenAI's dependence on Nvidia, with hardware chief Richard Ho, a former Google TPU lead, leading the effort.

title: Windows 10 quietly gets one more year of support and updates
blurb: Microsoft extended its Windows 10 Extended Security Updates program by a year, with the official support article now listing October 12, 2027 as the new end date. The free consumer ESU tier, first introduced when Windows 10 hit end of life in late 2025, will keep delivering security-only updates to enrolled users automatically, sparing hundreds of millions of PCs that can't move to Windows 11.

title: IBM reveals sub-1nm chip technology, production expected in another 5 years
blurb: IBM announced the first sub-1nm chip technology, a 0.7nm node using a "nanostack" architecture that vertically stacks and staggers nanosheet transistors to fit nearly 100 billion of them on a fingernail-sized chip. The research milestone promises up to 50% higher performance or 70% better energy efficiency over IBM's 2nm design, but production is at least five years out and depends on manufacturing partners, since IBM exited chipmaking years ago.

title: Databricks’ former AI chief thinks he can cut AI’s power bill by 1,000x
blurb: Unconventional AI, the startup led by former Databricks AI chief Naveen Rao, released Un-0, an image-generation model that matches state-of-the-art diffusion systems while running on a new oscillator-based computing architecture. Rao claims the approach could eventually cut inference power use by up to 1,000x; the sub-50-person company runs Un-0 on a software simulation today and plans to publish chip schematics soon.

title: Trump admin proposes axing brake-pedal requirement for AVs in a boost for Tesla
blurb: The Trump administration's DOT proposed eliminating the brake-pedal requirement for vehicles designed to run exclusively on automated driving systems, removing a major hurdle for fully autonomous cars without steering wheels or pedals. The change would clear the way for Tesla's two-seater Cybercab and Zoox's robotaxis to scale on public roads without per-vehicle FMVSS exemptions, and the public has 30 days to comment before the DOT decides.

title: China schedules Long March 10B rocket launch and recovery attempt
blurb: China set a July 10 launch window for the debut flight of its Long March 10B, a kerosene-liquid oxygen rocket that will attempt to recover its first stage at sea using a net-equipped vessel. The mission is the first orbital flight for the Long March 10 series, which is designed to carry Chinese astronauts to the moon, making it a critical early test for the program.

title: EU says AWS and Microsoft Azure should be treated as DMA gatekeepers
blurb: The European Commission told Amazon and Microsoft of its preliminary view that AWS and Azure should be designated gatekeepers under the Digital Markets Act, citing entrenched user bases, high switching costs, and lock-in. If the Commission confirms the finding, the two clouds would get six months to comply with DMA rules on interoperability and fair access, after launching the cloud-sector probe in November 2025.

title: Windows 11 can now turn back the clock when updates go bad
blurb: Microsoft added a "quick machine recovery" feature to Windows 11 that automatically rolls back updates when a device fails to boot after an install. The system detects boot failures, connects to the network in the recovery environment, and applies Microsoft-pushed fixes without user intervention, an effort to avoid a repeat of mass outages like the 2024 CrowdStrike incident.

title: Adobe acquires image and video enhancement tool maker Topaz Labs
blurb: Adobe acquired Topaz Labs, a two-decade-old maker of AI image and video enhancement tools known for its Astra upscaling and Wonder retouching models. Adobe will fold Topaz's models into Firefly and its editing suites, betting that on-device optimization and noise-reduction features keep users from defecting to rivals like Canva and Blackmagic's DaVinci Resolve. The deal is expected to close in the second half of 2026.

title: Chinese A.I. Models Gain Ground on Anthropic and OpenAI
blurb: Chinese startup Z.ai released AI models that approach Anthropic and OpenAI on capability while costing far less, drawing interest from Silicon Valley engineers testing the technology. The gap narrowing on price rather than raw performance signals that US labs' lead is increasingly defended by quality margins alone, not the cost-to-deploy advantage that once separated frontier models from cheaper alternatives.

title: How two Star Trek fans made three films using Unreal Engine and just an iPhone
blurb: A German fan studio shot three Star Trek films using Unreal Engine environments piped through an iPhone running Lightcraft Jetset as the viewfinder.

title: RAM crisis bites Apple as unprecedented Mac and iPad price rises arrive — cheapest MacBook Pro price hiked by $400 to $1,999
blurb: Apple raised prices across its entire Mac and iPad lineup, with the entry-level MacBook Pro jumping $400 to $1,999 and the iPad Pro climbing to $1,199 from $999. The company blamed surging memory and storage costs, telling Bloomberg it had shielded customers until now, while iPhone prices stay unchanged for the moment.

title: Apple takes over Swift Package Index, vows to remove  GitHub dependency
blurb: Apple has taken over the Swift Package Index, the community-run directory of open-source Swift packages, and committed to removing its dependency on GitHub. The move puts package hosting under Apple's control and signals a push to make the Swift ecosystem's core infrastructure independent of any single third-party platform.

title: Sources: Apple plans to skip higher-end M6 chips and launch its next Pro and Max chips in 2027 as part of the M7 lineup, to boost on-device AI capabilities (Mark Gurman/Bloomberg)
blurb: Apple plans to skip higher-end M6 chips and ship its next Pro and Max processors in 2027 as part of the M7 lineup, according to Bloomberg's Mark Gurman. The reported delay is aimed at boosting on-device AI capabilities, suggesting Apple is reworking its silicon roadmap to prioritize local inference over its usual annual high-end chip cadence.

title: Apple raises Mac, iPad, and other product prices by 15%-25%, saying it has "never seen a component price increase this much, this quickly"; iPhone is unchanged (Rolfe Winkler/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Apple raised prices on the Mac, iPad, and several other products by 15%-25%, citing component cost spikes it called unprecedented in speed and scale. The iPhone was left unchanged, suggesting Apple is shielding its highest-volume product from sticker shock while passing rising memory and parts costs onto the rest of its lineup.

title: AI Revenue Starts to Exceed a Key Infrastructure Cost But Not By Much
blurb: AI revenue at Meta, Alphabet, and Microsoft hit $25B, edging past the depreciation costs of the data center hardware running those workloads for the first time. The margin is thin, leaving Google and Amazon dependent on sustained premium pricing and strong user growth to recoup their massive capital outlays on AI infrastructure.

title: Apple stock drops 5% on MacBook and iPad price hikes due to memory crunch
blurb: Apple raised prices on MacBooks and iPads, its first move to pass surging memory and storage costs onto consumers, sending shares down more than 5% in their worst day since April 2025. The MacBook Neo entry jumped from $599 to $699 and the iPad Air 128GB from $599 to $749, with Apple blaming AI data center demand that has quadrupled memory prices over three quarters and leaving the door open to further hikes.

title: General Intuition, which trains AI agents in spatial reasoning via gameplay footage, raised $320M led by Khosla at a $2.3B valuation, for $454M in total funding (Rebecca Bellan/TechCrunch)
blurb: General Intuition raised $320M led by Khosla Ventures at a $2.3B valuation, bringing total funding to $454M to train AI agents in spatial reasoning using gameplay footage. The startup uses video game footage to teach agents navigation and physical-world reasoning, a bet that gaming data can solve the spatial-understanding gap that text-trained models still struggle with.

title: Qualcomm takes on NVIDIA with new Dragonfly CPU and AI chips
blurb: Qualcomm announced a data center lineup to challenge NVIDIA, anchored by the Dragonfly C1000 CPU with 250-plus custom Oryon cores and the Dragonfly AI300 inference accelerator. Meta signed a multi-year deal to use the C1000 in its next-generation server fleet, though the chip is not commercially available until 2028.

title: Optimizing cloud economics with linear elastic caching
blurb: Google researchers introduced linear elastic caching, which dynamically resizes in-memory caches by framing each page eviction as a ski rental problem and using lightweight machine learning to balance memory footprint against cache misses. Presented at CIDR, the approach treats memory as a variable cost that integrates over time rather than a fixed allocation, cutting total cost of ownership without sacrificing performance.

title: Apple passes RAMpocalypse costs on to consumers
blurb: Apple raised prices on Macs and iPads by hundreds of dollars, passing along the memory cost spike driven by surging DRAM demand. iPhones have been spared for now, but the increases signal how the industry-wide RAM shortage is reaching consumers as AI workloads soak up supply.

title: Amazon pours another $13B into India's AI and cloud infrastructure
blurb: Amazon committed another $13B to expand cloud and AI datacenter capacity in Mumbai and Hyderabad, part of a broader $48B five-year investment pledge in India. The spend deepens AWS's footprint in one of its fastest-growing markets as hyperscalers race to lock in regional capacity for AI workloads.

title: Ford AI hiccups push carmaker to rehire ‘gray beard’ inspectors
blurb: Ford has been rehiring veteran quality inspectors, known internally as "gray beards," after AI-based inspection systems failed to catch defects reliably on its assembly lines. The reversal underscores that automated vision systems still struggle with the judgment and edge-case recognition experienced humans bring, leaving manufacturers to pair AI tooling with the seasoned workers it was meant to replace.

title: Apple raises Mac and iPad prices, spares iPhone for now
blurb: Apple raised prices across its Mac and iPad lineups, blaming a worldwide memory shortage driven by the AI data-center buildout that has pushed DRAM and NAND costs up fourfold since Q4 2025. The MacBook Air now starts at $1,299 instead of $1,099 and the iPad Air jumped to $749 from $599, while the iPhone is spared for now though Apple says hikes there could land later this year.

title: Apple ratchets up prices, blames the cost of memory
blurb: Apple raised prices across most of its lineup today, adding $100 to $1,300 to Macs and iPads, with the M3 Ultra Mac Studio jumping to $5,299. CEO Tim Cook blamed soaring memory costs, as chipmakers prioritize the more profitable RAM used in AI data centers over consumer parts, leaving iPhone prices unchanged for now.

title: Sail, whose software optimizes how AI models run on existing chips, emerges from stealth with $80M in seed and Series A led by Kleiner at a $450M valuation (Lily Mae Lazarus/Fortune)
blurb: Sail emerged from stealth with $80M in combined seed and Series A funding, led by Kleiner Perkins at a $450M valuation. The startup's software optimizes how AI models run on existing chips, targeting the inference cost gap without requiring customers to buy new hardware.

title: DeepSeek Plans to Double Staff Across All Departments
blurb: DeepSeek plans to at least double headcount across every department, following agreements for a large new funding round. The Chinese AI lab is scaling up to compete with domestic rivals and global frontier labs including OpenAI and Anthropic, signaling a shift from research-driven lean operations to a broader commercial push.

title: OpenAI, Anthropic and SpaceX Are Shaking Up VC Fundraising
blurb: OpenAI, Anthropic, and SpaceX are driving record venture-capital fundraising as investors chase the outsized returns these companies promise. The scale of their valuations lets VC firms raise larger funds than ever, concentrating capital around a handful of AI and space bets while smaller startups compete for what remains.

title: Micron is up 13% after blockbuster earnings, but has pulled back from highs
blurb: Micron stock jumped as much as 19% Thursday after fiscal Q3 revenue more than quadrupled to $41.46 billion, beating estimates, briefly pushing its market cap above Meta and Tesla before gains pared to 13%. The memory maker guided to roughly $50 billion this quarter and locked in 16 long-term supply deals worth $22 billion, as AI data center demand tightens memory supply and lifts prices.

title: IBM Unveils New Method to Make Smaller Computer Chip Parts
blurb: IBM developed a new technique for fabricating smaller chip components, addressing industry fears that miniaturization had hit a physical wall. The advance could extend the gains in density and efficiency that have driven computing forward, suggesting that further shrinking of transistors remains viable rather than tapped out.

title: Here are all of the Apple price hikes
blurb: Apple raised prices across most of its hardware lineup today, including iPads, Macs, HomePods, Apple TV, Vision Pro, and the entry-level MacBook Neo, with increases ranging from $30 to $2,800 at the high end. The company blamed surging memory and storage costs driven by AI firms stockpiling RAM and SSDs, joining Nintendo, Sony, and Microsoft in passing the component crunch on to consumers.

title: Read Along in Google Classroom is now available to all education users to support foundational literacy
blurb: Google Classroom's Read Along is now live for all education users, offering real-time pronunciation help and reading-skill insights across eight languages.

title: Micron inks long-term supply agreements worth $100 billion — says it has no idea when RAM crisis will end
blurb: Micron signed 16 strategic customer agreements, 14 of which lock in roughly $100 billion in minimum-price revenue over five-year terms running 2026 to 2030. The deals include $22 billion in cash deposits and binding commitments as large buyers race to secure DRAM and 3D NAND supply, and CEO Sanjay Mehrotra warned that the memory shortage will not ease until 2028 at the earliest.

title: Apple will skip M6 Pro and M6 Max chips, new report says
blurb: Apple will skip the Pro and Max variants of its upcoming M6 chip, according to a Bloomberg report, making the M6 the first Apple Silicon Mac processor without higher-end configurations since 2020. The base M6 ships later this year with up to 200GB/s of memory bandwidth, an upgraded neural engine, and a redesigned 12-core GPU, before Apple pivots to an AI-focused M7 lineup next year.

title: Show HN: I made Google Trends for Hacker News by indexing 18 years of comments
blurb: A new Show HN project charts how often any topic, tool, or person has appeared on Hacker News, running live date-histograms over 45M posts and comments indexed across 18 years on Upstash Redis Search. Overlaying terms surfaces traction shifts over time, from the Webpack-to-Vite bundler handoff to the PyTorch-over-TensorFlow turn, with the underlying stories and comments listed below each chart and filterable by term or author.

title: Trump admin bars Polestar from selling its new EVs in the US
blurb: The Trump administration's Commerce Department barred Polestar from selling its new EVs in the US, denying the Geely-owned Swedish automaker a special authorization under the Connected Vehicle Rule that restricts cars with Chinese software or hardware. Polestar will sell off existing Polestar 3 and 4 stock and shift focus to Europe, which already accounted for the bulk of the 94% of Q1 sales it made outside the US.

title: Sources and a draft document: the US proposes that the EU sign on to an AI partnership to help secure chip supply chains; the one-page statement had few details (Alberto Nardelli/Bloomberg)
blurb: The US proposed that the EU join an AI partnership aimed at securing chip supply chains, according to sources and a draft document seen by Bloomberg. The one-page statement contained few concrete details, leaving open how the partnership would function or what commitments either side would make.

title: Amazon, Microsoft’s Cloud Services Should Fall Under DMA, EU Says
blurb: The EU said Amazon and Microsoft's cloud services should be designated as gatekeepers under the Digital Markets Act, subjecting them to obligations that prevent them from stifling competition. The classification would impose interoperability and fair-access requirements on AWS and Azure, expanding the DMA's reach beyond consumer platforms into enterprise cloud infrastructure for the first time.

title: Agility, Maker of Humanlike Robots, to Go Public in $2.5 Billion SPAC Deal
blurb: Agility Robotics agreed to go public through a SPAC merger valuing the maker of humanoid robots at $2.5 billion. The company's Digit robot already works in warehouses and manufacturing facilities for customers including Amazon, putting Agility among the first humanoid developers to reach public markets.

title: OpenAI's updated GPT-5.5 Instant is better at shopping, complex constraints, and understanding user intent  — and it's already in the API
blurb: OpenAI updated GPT-5.5 Instant, the default model in free ChatGPT, with improvements to shopping results, local recommendations, handling complex constraints, and inferring user intent. The company shared no benchmarks to quantify the gains, and it also pointed its chat-latest API alias at the new model while still recommending the separate gpt-5.5 model for production use.

title: Construction of 'world-changing' national supercomputer begins at Edinburgh University
blurb: Construction has begun on the UK's next national supercomputer at the University of Edinburgh, backed by a £750M government investment and built to run at least a billion billion calculations per second, roughly 50 times the power of the current ARCHER2 system. Owned by UK Research and Innovation, the machine targets cancer drug discovery, aircraft engineering, and extreme-weather modeling, and will route surplus heat into university buildings and possibly nearby homes.

title: Amazon ups India bet with fresh $13B AI infrastructure investment
blurb: Amazon will invest an additional $13B to expand AWS data center capacity in Mumbai and Hyderabad through 2030, announced after CEO Andy Jassy met Prime Minister Narendra Modi in New Delhi. The commitment is Amazon's third in three years and pushes its total India pledges to $48B, matching a broader rush by Microsoft, Google, and others betting the country becomes a major AI compute hub.

title: General Intuition’s $2.3B bet that video games can train AI agents for the real world
blurb: General Intuition raised $320M at a $2.3B valuation to train AI agents on video game footage, betting that gameplay teaches spatial reasoning that transfers to robots. The startup uses action-labeled clips from sibling company Medal, recording exactly which buttons players pressed, and says it fine-tuned a quadruped robot's navigation with just eight minutes of real-world data.

title: Small satellite operators confront a bottleneck to space access
blurb: SpaceX has stopped accepting Transporter rideshare reservations beyond late 2028, leaving at least nine satellite partners scrambling as the next two years of manifests fill up. With no competing provider matching SpaceX's frequency or roughly $8,000-per-kilogram pricing, small-sat makers now produce hardware faster than launch slots become available, a supply crunch that operators warn could threaten startups dependent on cheap, frequent access to orbit.

title: Researchers claim Microsoft's quantum breakthrough is flawed by basic Python errors
blurb: A critique published in Nature argues that Microsoft's 2025 claim of Majorana-based topological qubits rests on basic Python errors that discarded contradictory conductance data and surfaced only measurements supporting the topological-gap result. Microsoft rejects the criticism as a minor data-parsing oversight rather than a fatal flaw, but the dispute casts doubt on the foundational result behind its plan to ship a commercial quantum computer by 2029.

title: CoreWeave enlists Backblaze for massive cloud storage expansion
blurb: Backblaze secured a $335M, multi-exabyte deal to provide cloud storage for CoreWeave's managed storage infrastructure. The agreement adds to CoreWeave's existing $1.17B Vast Data contract, deepening the Nvidia-backed neocloud's storage stack as it scales data center capacity to handle high-throughput AI workloads.

title: Runpod raises $100M to build the leading cloud platform for AI developers
blurb: Runpod raised $100M in a Series A led by Summit Partners, reaching a $1B valuation and bringing total funding to $122M. The company pitches itself as an end-to-end "AI developer cloud" spanning experimentation, training, fine-tuning, and deployment, and says it has drawn over one million developers and served more than 20 billion inference requests since launch.

title: Cursor Deal Puts US On Track For Record Startup M&A Year
blurb: SpaceX's $60B acquisition of AI coding startup Cursor and parent Anysphere is the largest startup buyout ever, nearly double Google's $32B Wiz deal. The purchase alone accounts for roughly half of the $119.8B US acquirers have spent on venture-backed startups in 2026, putting the year on pace to exceed 2025's record M&A tally.

title: New effort will get genome sequences for entire Endangered Species list
blurb: The US government and Colossal Biosciences announced a partnership to sequence genomes and preserve tissue samples for all 2,300-plus species on the Endangered Species list. The pairing is notable given the administration's broader push to weaken the Act and Colossal's contested record on de-extinction, including a dire wolf claim built on only a handful of edits to grey wolf DNA.

title: Samsung Joins With US Battery Startup Seeking Pentagon Cash
blurb: Samsung SDI agreed to invest $20M and provide engineering support to Forge Nano, a US battery startup building cells for the defense and aerospace industries at a new North Carolina plant. The deal positions Forge Nano to pursue Pentagon funding while giving Samsung a stake in domestic US battery manufacturing aimed at military and aviation customers.

title: Sail raises $80M to make AI agents cheaper to run
blurb: Sail Research raised $80M in combined seed and Series A funding at a $450M valuation to cut the cost of running AI agents, with Sequoia leading the seed and Kleiner Perkins the Series A. Founded by ex-Apple and ex-NVIDIA engineers, the startup rebuilt its inference stack for throughput over latency and claims up to 10x lower cost per token for agents that burn billions of tokens on a single task.

title: Google Play updates let U.K. developers do more and pay less.
blurb: Google Play will lower service fees and let U.K. developers use their own billing systems or send users to their websites for digital purchases, starting June 30. The changes, which address concerns raised by the U.K.'s CMA, also add a Registered App Stores program later this year and lower rates through revamped developer programs from September 30.

title: Google Finance exits beta with a dedicated Android app, portfolio tracking, and AI-powered scheduled briefings
blurb: Google Finance exited beta with a dedicated Android app, portfolio tracking, an AI research tool, and scheduled market briefings, with an iOS version due later this year. The release restores the portfolio feature Google cut in 2017 and adds Gemini-powered tools that explain stock moves, answer questions about a user's holdings, and deliver custom briefings on a set schedule.

title: Google will open the Play Store to outside billing on June 30
blurb: Google will open the Play Store to outside billing on June 30 in the US, UK, and Europe, and cut its commission from 30% to a 10% service fee on a developer's first $1M in annual earnings. The change settles Google's court battle with Epic Games, letting developers route users to their own payment systems while Play's billing adds a 5% fee; the policy goes worldwide by September 2027.

title: A coalition of publishers sued OpenAI and Microsoft over scraping content without consent
blurb: A coalition of publishers owning nearly 400 newspapers sued OpenAI and Microsoft in the Southern District of New York, alleging the companies scraped articles, including paywalled content, to train ChatGPT and Copilot without permission or payment. The suit seeks statutory damages and injunctive relief under copyright law and the DMCA, marking one of the largest publisher coalitions yet to challenge AI firms over training data.

title: Microsoft updates Visual Studio Code with chat cost tracking and multi-agent chats
blurb: Microsoft released Visual Studio Code 1.126, adding whole-session chat cost tracking and the ability to run multiple side-by-side chats within a single Copilot agent session. The update also opens untrusted folders directly in Restricted Mode without a pop-up, and the cost view arrives as free AI tiers in IDEs give way to credit-based usage.

title: Meta Launches Cheaper Smart Glasses Without Ray-Ban, Starting at $299
blurb: Meta released its own line of AI smart glasses, the Adventurer and Fury, starting at $299 without Ray-Ban branding and $80 below last year's Wayfarer model. EssilorLuxottica still handles manufacturing, and Meta added a $399 Starfire model with Kylie Jenner while weighing a cheaper camera-free version, as it pushes to prove its AI investments can yield consumer hits.

title: ARX Robotics and Roboneers form ARX Industries to scale unmanned ground vehicle production
blurb: ARX Robotics of Germany and Ukraine's Roboneers formed ARX Industries, a joint venture to mass-produce the Rys Pro unmanned ground vehicle across facilities in both countries. The venture targets thousands of units in its first year and tens of thousands annually after, supporting Ukraine's goal of fielding 50,000 UGVs in 2026 for roles spanning casualty evacuation, frontline logistics, and demining.

title: Hey Nico, you didn't vibe code your data room but stole it from Papermark
blurb: Papermark founder Marc Seitz publicly accused YC founder Nico Laqua of copying Papermark's open-source and enterprise-licensed code to build a competing data room product, demanding he take it down and framing it as fraud rather than legitimate building. The post, which copies in YC's Garry Tan and the wider community, highlights how open-source licensing disputes and AI-era "vibe coding" claims are colliding over what counts as theft versus inspiration.

title: Building AI tailored for education, with educators in the lead
blurb: Google is expanding teacher-led AI activities in Google Classroom, letting educators build curriculum-grounded study guides, adaptive quizzes, and homework support across Guided Learning in Gemini, study notebooks, and NotebookLM. Teachers select which class materials inform each activity and get individual and class-level insights into student progress, with the tools rolling out in the coming months to Classroom and select learning management systems.

title: Netris raises $15M Series A from a16z to help AI neoclouds go live faster
blurb: Netris raised $15M in a Series A led by a16z to automate network setup for AI neoclouds, the smaller operators racing to rent out GPU capacity. Its hardware-accelerated software configures switches and handles multi-tenancy, cutting the months of work needed to bring a cluster online and reducing the cost of idle GPUs while a data center sits unconfigured.

title: OpenAI, Anthropic and Amazon are funding RAISE US
blurb: OpenAI, Anthropic, Amazon, and Microsoft are funding RAISE US, a new nonprofit that has raised over $500M to retrain American workers for the AI economy. Led by former commerce secretary Gina Raimondo with a $1B target, the coalition unites four fierce model-building rivals plus IBM, Mastercard, AMD, and others to help governors and employers manage AI-driven job disruption.

title: Feds deny Polestar authorization to sell cars in US from model year 2027
blurb: The US Commerce Department denied Polestar authorization to import model year 2027 and later vehicles, citing a rule that bars connected cars from automakers with Chinese ties. The Geely-owned EV brand will sell remaining Polestar 3 and 4 stock but cannot bring the Polestar 5, 6, or future models stateside, even though the Polestar 3 is built at Volvo's South Carolina plant.

title: Russia allegedly used a forensics platform to hack an activist's phone, despite having its access cut off
blurb: Citizen Lab found that Russian authorities used Cellebrite's UFED forensic tools to extract data from jailed activist Andrey Pivovarov's iPhone 12 in 2021, months after Cellebrite cut off its Russian customers. The case shows that revoking software contracts does little to stop a state from running existing forensic hardware, which pulled messages from WhatsApp, Telegram, and Viber to build a prosecution.

title: Xbox prices spike another $100 or more
blurb: Microsoft will raise Xbox console prices again on August 1st, adding $100 to 512GB models and $150 to 1TB models, pushing the Series S to $499.99 and the disc-drive Series X to $799.99. The company blames a 2.5x jump in storage and memory costs, is sunsetting the 2TB Series X, and is adding financing and used-console programs to soften the hikes.

title: How KRAFTON Built PUBG Ally, a Co-Playable Character Powered by NVIDIA ACE
blurb: KRAFTON built PUBG Ally, an AI teammate for PUBG: BATTLEGROUNDS that runs on NVIDIA ACE using speech recognition, an on-device 2B-parameter language model, and text-to-speech to understand voice commands and react to game events in real time. KRAFTON calls it a co-playable character that cooperates, adapts, and remembers across sessions, distinct from a scripted NPC, and it entered public beta in Arcade Mode on June 17.

title: Notion plans to shut down its Gmail client Notion Mail on September 22 and go "all in" on AI agents to run inboxes, saying 50%+ of users do not open the inbox (Zac Hall/9to5Mac)
blurb: Notion will shut down Notion Mail, its Gmail client, on September 22 and shift entirely to AI agents that manage inboxes on users' behalf. The company says more than 50% of users never open the inbox view, justifying a bet that agents, not a redesigned mail UI, are the future of email.

title: Nvidia Wants to Make Humanoid AI Robots Safer Around Humans
blurb: Nvidia is building safety technology that lets humanoid robots recognize hazards and react in real time before working alongside people. Robotics VP Deepu Talla said the push centers on edge AI that can detect danger instantly, treating split-second hazard response as the gating requirement for deploying humanoids in factories and other human environments.

title: Cerebras On Track for Record Two-Day Loss as Outlook Disappoints
blurb: Cerebras shares tumbled Thursday and were on track for a record two-session loss after the AI chipmaker issued a weaker-than-expected annual sales forecast. The disappointing outlook rattled investors who had bet on the company riding surging demand for AI inference hardware, marking a sharp reversal for one of the sector's most-watched Nvidia challengers.

title: Alphabet Shares Fall After Report on Further AI Talent Departures
blurb: Alphabet shares fell after a report of additional departures among its AI talent, deepening a selloff that began last week when several AI architects left the company. The losses underscore intensifying competition for top researchers, as rival labs and well-funded startups continue to poach the engineers driving frontier model development.

title: Applied Materials unveils more advanced chipmaking gear for 3D stacking architectures
blurb: Applied Materials introduced new chipmaking systems for advanced packaging, process control, and DRAM manufacturing aimed at the 3D-stacking architectures behind next-generation AI processors. The lineup, including the Opta Quad CMP platform and Nokota Vmax 2 ECD system, targets the defects and yield losses that plague stacking thin DRAM dies with through-silicon vias, the key bottleneck in scaling past the memory wall.

title: You may see new ways to pay for Android apps and subscriptions starting next week
blurb: Starting June 30, Google will let developers in the US, UK, and Europe offer alternative payment systems for Android apps and subscriptions instead of requiring Google Play Billing. Developers who keep using Play Billing pay an added 5% fee while those who route users elsewhere avoid it, though whether any savings reach consumers depends on each developer, with the rollout expanding to Australia, Japan, and South Korea through 2027.

title: Microsoft Raises Xbox Console Prices for Third Time in 13 Months
blurb: Microsoft raised prices on its current-generation Xbox consoles for the third time in 13 months, citing component shortages that have pushed up costs across consumer tech. The repeated hikes underscore how supply-chain pressure on chips and memory is now reaching end customers, eroding the console-as-loss-leader model that Microsoft and rivals have long used to drive game and subscription sales.

title: Here is every Apple product that hasn’t yet increased in price
blurb: Apple rolled out price increases across most of its lineup today, sparing only its newest iPhone, Apple Watch, and AirPods models, which total seven iPhones, three Watches, and four AirPods. Macs, iPads, Home products, and Vision Pro are all more expensive effective immediately, with Tim Cook attributing the hikes to AI-driven RAM and storage constraints that he called unavoidable last week.

title: Apple hikes prices for most of its hardware
blurb: Apple raised prices across most of its Mac, iPad, and accessory lineup, with hikes ranging from $100 on the entry-level MacBook Neo and basic iPad to $1,300 on the M3 Ultra Mac Studio. The move follows Tim Cook's warning that Apple can no longer absorb the memory crunch, though iPhone and AirPods prices are so far untouched.

title: Anthropic hires French telecom group Orange's Chief AI Officer Steve Jarrett to help Anthropic "better understand and adapt" its products to Europe and Africa (Gianluca Lo Nostro/Reuters)
blurb: Anthropic hired Steve Jarrett, chief AI officer at French telecom group Orange, to lead its product expansion across Europe and Africa. The hire signals Anthropic is investing in regional adaptation as it competes with OpenAI and Google for enterprise customers outside the US market.

title: Micron's AI Pricing Power Explained
blurb: Micron's pricing power in memory chips has surged as AI demand turns DRAM and high-bandwidth memory into a strategic, supply-constrained asset. D.A. Davidson analyst Gil Luria argues the shift may be permanent, and that Apple could emerge stronger despite higher chip costs by absorbing them better than rivals.

title: Micron reveals AI companies are spending billions to lock up its memory years in advance
blurb: Micron signed 16 multi-year supply agreements backed by roughly $22 billion in customer deposits and commitments, locking in DRAM and NAND deliveries that run as far out as 2030. The deals could generate around $100 billion in future contracted obligations and cover about 20% of expected DRAM shipments and a third of NAND, as AI demand for high-bandwidth memory pushes buyers to prepay for guaranteed supply.

title: ASUS beta BIOS updates restore Ryzen 9000 memory encryption ahead of AMD’s July timeline — TSME returns to select AM5 boards after silent backlash over removal
blurb: ASUS started rolling out beta BIOS updates restoring TSME memory encryption to AM5 boards, ahead of AMD's July fix for Ryzen 9000.

title: The Unbearable Cheapness of Open Weight Models
blurb: The author argues that open-weight models like DeepSeek V4 and Xiaomi's Mimo now undercut Anthropic and OpenAI frontier APIs by 20-50x on token pricing, leaving the incumbents stuck defending high margins they can't easily cut. He warns that the likely response is manufactured scarcity through luxury-style "frontier" gating and China-fear lobbying for open-weight bans, and points to Allen AI's fully open OLMo as the counterweight worth backing.

title: Apple Store is down: ‘We’re making updates,’ Apple says [Update: Back online]
blurb: Apple's Online Store went down Thursday morning and returned with across-the-board price increases on Macs, iPads, and other products. The outage coincides with Apple's annual Back to School promotion prep and follows Tim Cook's warning last week that rising component costs would push prices higher.

title: Gates Foundation backs Leyden Labs’ €40M raise: Can a nasal spray do what 80 years of flu vaccines couldn’t
blurb: Leyden Labs raised €40M to push PanFlu, a nasal spray that delivers broadly protective monoclonal antibodies to the nose and throat, into clinical trials. The Gates Foundation joined as a first-time backer alongside returning investors the EIC Fund, Invest-NL, and ClavystBio, with a mandate to expand access in low- and middle-income countries, bringing Leyden's total funding past $336M.

title: Google Finance gets a dedicated app for Android
blurb: Google launched a dedicated Google Finance app on Android, bundling watchlists, real-time market data, live financial news, and an AI "Key Moments" feature that explains stock movements. The move pushes Google into direct competition with Yahoo Finance and Robinhood, with an iOS version, live earnings-call audio, and AI-driven portfolios and natural-language task briefings rolling out in the coming months.

title: This $5 Billion Startup Is Putting Humanoid Robots To Work
blurb: A $5 billion startup is deploying humanoid robots into working roles at sites in Texas, moving the machines from demos into commercial operations. The rollout tests whether general-purpose humanoids can handle real industrial tasks at scale, a key question for a sector betting that physical labor is the next frontier for AI.

title: US Customs Ramps Up AI Investment in Push to Sharpen Enforcement
blurb: US Customs and Border Protection is expanding its AI investment to catch tariff dodgers and flag illicit cargo before shipments reach US ports. The push comes as the agency faces pressure to enforce President Trump's escalating trade war, betting that machine learning can spot fraudulent declarations and high-risk goods faster than manual inspection.

title: Wall Street isn’t buying Apple’s unprecedented price hikes on products
blurb: Apple raised prices across Macs, iPads, and other existing products, blaming rising RAM costs, and the stock fell 5% in response. The timing lets incoming CEO John Ternus avoid owning the increase and softens the blow when new Siri AI products arrive needing more memory and carrying higher prices of their own.

title: Apple is ‘working tirelessly’ to find solutions to component shortage as it hikes prices across Mac, iPad and more
blurb: Apple raised prices across Mac, iPad, Apple TV, and HomePod after memory and storage component costs hit unprecedented levels, calling the move "not welcome news" while it works "tirelessly to find solutions." The iPhone was spared for now, but Morgan Stanley expects roughly $50 increases on the iPhone 18 Pro this fall, partly offset by Apple's switch from Qualcomm to in-house modems.

title: Notion shutting down its AI-powered email client, including Mac and iOS apps
blurb: Notion is shutting down Notion Mail, its AI-powered email client, across web, desktop, and iOS on September 22, just over a year after the April 2025 launch. The company said more than half of Notion Mail users already manage email without opening their inbox, and it is redirecting the effort toward AI agents that run inboxes automatically.

title: Microsoft raises Xbox console prices by up to $150, discontinues 2TB version
blurb: Microsoft will raise Xbox Series X|S prices by $100 to $150 starting August 1, 2026, and discontinue all 2TB models, citing a memory and storage cost crunch that has pushed component prices up more than 2.5x since its last hike. To soften the increase, the company is adding buy-now-pay-later plans, 0% APR financing, trade-in programs, and certified refurbished consoles at up to $100 off.

title: AMD Ryzen 7 5800X3D re-review: Maxing out DDR4’s gaming potential
blurb: AMD re-released the Ryzen 7 5800X3D at $350, still the top DDR4 gaming chip but tough to justify outside AM4.

title: DeepReinforce releases Ornith-1.0 open-source coding models
blurb: DeepReinforce open-sourced Ornith-1.0, a self-improving family of agentic coding models spanning a 9B Dense build for edge use up to a 397B MoE flagship, each trained on Gemma 4 and Qwen 3.5 foundations. The models learn to write their own task-specific scaffolds alongside solutions, with the 397B version reportedly hitting 77.5 on Terminal-Bench 2.1 and 82.4 on SWE-Bench Verified.

title: Anthropic accuses Alibaba of campaign to 'brazenly' and 'illicitly' extract AI capabilities
blurb: Anthropic sent a letter to the U.S. Senate Banking Committee accusing Alibaba of "brazenly" and "illicitly" attempting to extract its AI capabilities through what it called the largest known distillation attack against the company to date. Anthropic said operators affiliated with Alibaba's AI lab ran 28.8 million exchanges across roughly 25,000 fraudulent accounts between April 22 and June 5.

title: New Gaslight macOS Malware Uses Prompt Injection to Disrupt AI-Assisted Analysis
blurb: SentinelOne identified Gaslight, a Rust-based macOS implant and information stealer that embeds a prompt injection payload designed to make LLM-assisted triage agents doubt their session and abort analysis of the artifact. Attributed with high confidence to North Korea-aligned actors, the malware uses a Telegram bot C2 channel, a six-command interactive shell, and a LaunchAgent for persistence.

title: Interesting Paper Exploring Prompt Injection
blurb: This paper argues that prompt injection succeeds because LLMs recognize roles by the style of text in instruction blocks rather than the role tags themselves, meaning the role boundaries that separate instructions from data never survive into the model's internal representations. The authors conclude that injection defense will remain whack-a-mole until models achieve genuine role perception, and warn that continuous role boundaries enable attacks that subtly shift model state through innocuous-looking text.

title: Unconventional AI releases its first model, built on an oscillator architecture its founder says could cut power use by a factor of a thousand
blurb: Unconventional AI released Un-0, an image generation model running on a simulated oscillator architecture that founder Naveen Rao says could eventually cut AI power consumption by 1,000x. The model matches state-of-the-art diffusion systems like Stable Diffusion in an accompanying paper, but it runs on a software simulation of coupled ring oscillators rather than physical hardware, which does not yet exist.

title: Qualcomm reveals HBC near-memory AI architecture, AI250 and AI350 accelerators — touts 6x higher bandwidth-per-watt compared to HBM, 200x capacity compared to on-chip SRAM
blurb: Qualcomm introduced High-Bandwidth Compute (HBC), a near-memory architecture that disaggregates the AI accelerator from the SoC and places it beneath an LPDDR DRAM stack connected via through-silicon vias, powering its new AI250 and AI350 data center accelerators. The design claims 6x higher bandwidth-per-watt than HBM and over 200x the capacity of on-chip SRAM, dropping the costly silicon interposer, though Qualcomm has not disclosed actual bandwidth figures.

title: Qualcomm acquires AI startup Modular in open ecosystem bet to challenge CUDA
blurb: Qualcomm acquired Modular, the AI infrastructure startup founded by LLVM and Swift creator Chris Lattner, to build a hardware-agnostic compute layer that challenges Nvidia's CUDA lock-in. Modular's "write once, run anywhere" stack ports models across Nvidia, AMD, Arm, and Apple silicon, and gives Qualcomm software depth as it pushes into data center AI infrastructure.

title: Anthropic claims that China's Alibaba used 25,000 fake accounts and 28.8 million exchanges to illicitly 'distill' its Claude model — violations occurred from April to June 2026
blurb: Anthropic told the U.S. Senate Banking Committee that operators tied to Alibaba and its Qwen lab used 25,000 fake accounts and 28.8 million exchanges to illicitly distill Claude between April and June 2026. The letter follows earlier Anthropic accusations against DeepSeek, Moonshot, and MiniMax, and warns that distillation could help China cheaply build frontier models rivaling U.S. systems.

title: Scaling AI Inference Across Multiple GPUs Using NVIDIA TensorRT with Multi-Device Inference Support
blurb: TensorRT 11.0 introduces multi-device inference support, bringing native high-performance multi-GPU inference to the TensorRT runtime while preserving kernel fusions, memory planning, and quantization. The feature integrates directly with NVIDIA NCCL for transport optimization across NVLink, PCIe, and InfiniBand, and supports tensor and context parallelism so PyTorch models can be deployed via Torch-TensorRT beyond single-device memory limits.

title: Apple to Skip High-End M6 Mac Chips in Favor of AI-Focused M7 Line
blurb: Apple plans to skip high-end M6 Mac chips and jump to an AI-focused M7 generation, launching M7 Pro, M7 Max, and M7 Ultra processors for its top-end Macs. The move marks one of the largest shifts in Apple's silicon strategy, prioritizing on-device AI performance over the incremental cadence that has defined its M-series rollouts.

title: EU Held Talks With White House on Anthropic After Mythos Cutoff
blurb: The European Union held talks with the Trump administration over access to Anthropic's most advanced AI models after the US restricted foreign nationals from using the technology earlier this month under its Mythos cutoff. The discussions reflect growing tension over US export controls on frontier AI, which now limit allied governments and companies from reaching top-tier systems.

title: Anthropic accuses Alibaba of using 25,000 fake accounts to scrape Claude AI
blurb: Anthropic accused Alibaba of running nearly 25,000 fraudulent accounts to extract capabilities from its Claude models, calling it the largest known attack of its kind against the company. The campaign ran between April 22 and June 5, generated over 28.8 million exchanges, and used distillation to train a weaker model on Claude's outputs, following similar efforts Anthropic attributed to DeepSeek, Moonshot AI, and MiniMax.

title: OpenAI unveils first chip as part of Broadcom deal in effort to 'build the full stack'
blurb: OpenAI and Broadcom released Jalapeño, OpenAI's first custom AI chip, which Broadcom will manufacture for OpenAI to run inference across ChatGPT and its other products. President Greg Brockman said the chip was designed end to end in nine months with help from OpenAI's own models, part of a broader plan to deploy OpenAI-designed racks requiring 10 gigawatts of power.

title: The engineer who built Swift and LLVM sold his AI startup to Qualcomm for $4B
blurb: Qualcomm is acquiring Modular, the AI infrastructure startup co-founded by LLVM and Swift creator Chris Lattner, in an all-stock deal worth roughly $3.92B. The acquisition gives Qualcomm a hardware-agnostic platform that lets developers run AI workloads across Nvidia, AMD, and Apple chips without rewriting code, already used by Oracle, Amazon, Nvidia, and AMD.

title: Salesforce unveils AI Help Agent with pay-per-resolution pricing
blurb: Salesforce announced Agentforce Help Agent, a pre-packaged service agent that connects to an organization's knowledge base to resolve customer and employee issues across chat, portals, and voice channels. It is Salesforce's first product to use pay-per-resolution pricing, charging only when the agent fully resolves an issue without escalating to a human or being abandoned.

title: Sources: Google expands the scope of its months-old AI coding strike team to "midtraining" to try to catch up with Anthropic, after major executive departures (Erin Woo/The Information)
blurb: Google expanded its months-old AI coding strike team to cover "midtraining" as it works to close the gap with Anthropic on coding models, according to The Information. The reorganization follows major executive departures and reflects pressure on Google to compete with Anthropic's Claude in the increasingly lucrative AI coding market.

title: New macOS malware embeds fake errors to confuse AI analysis tools
blurb: SentinelOne discovered a macOS malware family it calls Gaslight that embeds prompt injection strings and 38 fake system-failure messages, including fabricated memory dumps and SQL injection alerts, to make LLM-assisted analysis tools doubt their own session and abort. The Rust backdoor, attributed with high confidence to a North Korean-linked actor, targets the analysis agent's perception rather than the sandbox it runs in.

title: Adobe Acquires AI Upscaling Specialists Topaz Labs
blurb: Adobe announced it will acquire Topaz Labs, the Dallas-based maker of AI upscaling, denoising, and sharpening tools like Gigapixel and Topaz Photo. Adobe will integrate Topaz's enhancement models across Photoshop, Lightroom, and Firefly to compete with Canva and DaVinci Resolve, with the deal expected to close in the second half of 2026.

title: Qualcomm plans China-specific data center chips — new Dragonfly lineup will include nerfed AI accelerators that comply with export thresholds
blurb: Qualcomm will bring all four of its Dragonfly data center product lines to China, including AI accelerators engineered to stay below U.S. export thresholds, CEO Cristiano Amon told Nikkei Asia. The first accelerator, the AI250, ships next year using a HBC near-memory design rather than the tight-supply HBM stacks Nvidia and AMD rely on, targeting a market where buyers are being steered toward Huawei and Cambricon.

title: AI and Liability
blurb: A German court ruled that Google is liable for its AI search summaries, rejecting the defense that users know not to trust AI-generated information blindly. Schneier argues AI overviews break the carrier-versus-publisher distinction that has long shielded internet platforms, since they rewrite third-party content and exercise editorial discretion rather than merely quoting and linking to it.

title: Building a Cluster-Aware AI Agent with Kubernetes, Argo CD, and GitOps
blurb: This walkthrough builds a self-hosted, read-only AI agent that runs entirely inside a Kubernetes cluster, pairing an Ollama-served Mistral 7B model with a FastAPI service that reads live cluster state through a scoped ServiceAccount. The agent's /diagnose endpoint grounds its reasoning in real pod events and logs rather than training data alone, while GitHub Actions and Argo CD Image Updater handle the full GitOps delivery chain with no data leaving the cluster.

title: Which tokens does a hybrid model predict better?
blurb: Allen AI compared its 7B Olmo 3 transformer against Olmo Hybrid, matched on data, tokenizer, and training recipe so prediction differences isolate the architecture itself. The hybrid wins on meaning-carrying tokens like nouns, verbs, and pronoun references that require tracking context, while the transformer holds its edge on tokens copied verbatim from earlier in the input.

title: Databricks’ former AI chief thinks he can cut AI’s power bill by 1,000x
blurb: Unconventional AI, led by former Databricks AI head Naveen Rao, released Un-0, an image-generation model that matches state-of-the-art diffusion systems while running on a simulation of a new oscillator-based computer architecture. Rao claims the architecture could eventually cut inference power use by up to 1,000x, and the company plans to release chip schematics soon and build out a full inference stack.

title: OpenAI gets spicy with Broadcom via AI data movement-optimized ‘Jalapeño’ chip
blurb: OpenAI received its first custom chip, "Jalapeño," an inference accelerator co-designed with Broadcom and optimized to reduce data movement while balancing compute, memory, and networking. The chip went from design to tape-out in roughly nine months and is framed as the first step in a multi-generation compute platform informed by OpenAI products like ChatGPT and Codex.

title: Adobe acquires image and video enhancement tool maker Topaz Labs
blurb: Adobe acquired Topaz Labs, the two-decade-old maker of AI image and video enhancement tools, and will fold its models into Firefly and its broader Creative Cloud editing suites. Topaz brings Astra for video upscaling, Wonder for image retouching, and on-device optimization that runs large video models on consumer GPUs, helping Adobe fend off Canva and Blackmagic. The deal is expected to close in the second half of 2026.

title: Chinese A.I. Models Gain Ground on Anthropic and OpenAI
blurb: Chinese AI company Z.ai has released models that approach the quality of Anthropic and OpenAI systems at a fraction of the cost, drawing interest from Silicon Valley engineers. The price gap is pushing US developers to test Chinese alternatives, narrowing the performance lead that American labs have held over their Chinese competitors.

title: New Linux Foundation project aims to bring DNS-style trust to AI agents
blurb: The Linux Foundation announced the Agent Name Service, a framework that extends DNS to establish identity, ownership, and trust for AI agents across enterprise systems. Built on existing domains enterprises already control, ANS lets agents and systems verify who an agent represents, what permissions it holds, and whether its code and history remain authentic, without relying on any proprietary registry or centralized control.

title: AI Revenue Starts to Exceed a Key Infrastructure Cost But Not By Much
blurb: AI revenue at Meta, Alphabet, and Microsoft hit $25 billion, narrowly topping the depreciation costs of the data center infrastructure powering it. The margin is thin enough that Google and Amazon will need to sustain premium pricing and strong user growth to recoup their massive capital investments.

title: Qualcomm takes on NVIDIA with new Dragonfly CPU and AI chips
blurb: Qualcomm announced a data center lineup to challenge NVIDIA, including the 250-core Dragonfly C1000 CPU and the Dragonfly AI300 rack-level inference platform. The company claims the AI300 delivers 4x to 8x better performance per watt than existing GPU architectures, and Meta signed a multi-year agreement to use C1000 CPUs in its next-generation server fleet.

title: Amazon pours another $13B into India's AI and cloud infrastructure
blurb: Amazon announced an additional $13B investment to expand AI and cloud infrastructure in India, including new datacenters in Mumbai and Hyderabad. The spending is part of a broader $48B five-year pledge aimed at meeting rising demand for AWS compute capacity across the Indian market.

title: New MCP specification kills old risks but opens fresh attack surfaces, Akamai finds
blurb: Akamai's analysis of the upcoming MCP 2026-07-28 specification finds that the overhaul removes several protocol-level security risks while shifting responsibility for others onto developers. The rebuild eliminates protocol-managed sessions and the Mcp-Session-Id hijacking vector, restricts server-initiated prompts, and mandates OAuth 2.1 with PKCE, but its new stateless model lets clients hold workflow state, exposing deployments to hijacking when servers use predictable tracking IDs or skip state-object validation.

title: Sail, whose software optimizes how AI models run on existing chips, emerges from stealth with $80M in seed and Series A led by Kleiner at a $450M valuation (Lily Mae Lazarus/Fortune)
blurb: Sail emerged from stealth with $80M in seed and Series A funding led by Kleiner Perkins at a $450M valuation, building software that optimizes how AI models run on existing chips. The company targets better performance from current hardware rather than new silicon, positioning its software layer as a way to ease compute constraints without waiting on next-generation chips.

title: DeepSeek Plans to Double Staff Across All Departments
blurb: DeepSeek said it plans to at least double the size of every department, following agreements for a large new fundraising round. The expansion is aimed at strengthening its position against domestic Chinese rivals and global leaders including OpenAI and Anthropic.

title: Sources: Apple plans to skip higher-end M6 chips and launch its next Pro and Max chips in 2027 as part of the M7 lineup, to boost on-device AI capabilities (Mark Gurman/Bloomberg)
blurb: Apple plans to skip the higher-end M6 chips and instead launch its next Pro and Max processors in 2027 as part of the M7 lineup, according to Bloomberg's Mark Gurman. The shift is aimed at boosting on-device AI capabilities, suggesting Apple is reworking its chip roadmap around local inference rather than incremental performance gains.

title: Ford AI hiccups push carmaker to rehire ‘gray beard’ inspectors
blurb: Ford has been rehiring veteran quality inspectors, known internally as "gray beards," after AI-based inspection systems failed to catch defects reliably on its production lines. The reversal underscores the limits of replacing experienced human judgment in manufacturing, where seasoned workers detect subtle flaws that automated vision systems still miss.

title: General Intuition, which trains AI agents in spatial reasoning via gameplay footage, raised $320M led by Khosla at a $2.3B valuation, for $454M in total funding (Rebecca Bellan/TechCrunch)
blurb: General Intuition raised $320M led by Khosla Ventures at a $2.3B valuation, bringing total funding to $454M, to train AI agents in spatial reasoning using gameplay footage. The company uses video from games to teach agents navigation and physical-world reasoning, capabilities it argues are underdeveloped in models trained primarily on text.

title: Sources and a draft document: the US proposes that the EU sign on to an AI partnership to help secure chip supply chains; the one-page statement had few details (Alberto Nardelli/Bloomberg)
blurb: The US proposed that the EU join an AI partnership aimed at securing semiconductor supply chains, according to sources and a draft document seen by Bloomberg. The one-page statement contained few specifics, leaving the scope, funding, and obligations of the proposed arrangement unclear.

title: Micron soars 16% after blockbuster earnings, lifting some chip stocks
blurb: Micron reported fiscal third-quarter revenue of $41.46 billion, more than quadrupling from $9.3 billion a year earlier and beating analyst estimates of nearly $36 billion as AI data center demand drives a memory shortage. The company guided to roughly $50 billion for the current quarter and has locked in 16 long-term supply agreements worth $22 billion in commitments, briefly pushing its market cap above Meta and Tesla.

title: General Intuition’s $2.3B bet that video games can train AI agents for the real world
blurb: General Intuition raised $320M at a $2.3B valuation to build agentic models that generalize from video game footage to robotics, bringing its total disclosed funding to $454M. The startup, spun out of gameplay-clip platform Medal, trains on action labels recording exactly which buttons players pressed, and says it fine-tuned a quadruped robot's controller with just eight minutes of real-world data.

title: OpenAI's updated GPT-5.5 Instant is better at shopping, complex constraints, and understanding user intent  — and it's already in the API
blurb: OpenAI updated GPT-5.5 Instant, the default model in free ChatGPT, with improvements to shopping results, local recommendations, intent understanding, and handling complex constraints, though it has not released benchmarks to quantify the gains. The update rolls out to paid subscribers first and free users on June 25, and OpenAI pointed its chat-latest API alias at the model while still recommending the separate gpt-5.5 model for production.

title: Cursor Deal Puts US On Track For Record Startup M&A Year
blurb: SpaceX's $60B acquisition of AI coding startup Cursor and parent company Anysphere has put US startup M&A on pace to break 2025's record, with acquirers spending at least $119.8B so far in 2026. The Cursor deal is the largest startup acquisition ever, nearly double Google's $32B purchase of Wiz, and accounts for roughly half of this year's total M&A spending.

title: Building AI tailored for education, with educators in the lead
blurb: Google is expanding teacher-led AI activities in Google Classroom, bringing curriculum-grounded Guided Learning, study notebooks, and NotebookLM to educators in the coming months.

title: Runpod raises $100M to build the leading cloud platform for AI developers
blurb: Runpod raised $100M in Series A funding led by Summit Partners at a $1B valuation, bringing its total funding to $122M. The cloud platform lets developers experiment, train, fine-tune, and deploy models on one dashboard, and has served over 20B inference requests across more than one million developers since launch.

title: A coalition of publishers sued OpenAI and Microsoft over scraping content without consent
blurb: A coalition of publishers owning nearly 400 newspapers sued OpenAI and Microsoft, alleging the companies scraped their articles, including paywalled content, to train ChatGPT and Copilot without compensation. Filed in the Southern District of New York, the suit seeks statutory damages and injunctive relief for copyright infringement and DMCA violations, while OpenAI maintains its models are trained on public data under fair use.

title: Meta Launches Cheaper Smart Glasses Without Ray-Ban, Starting at $299
blurb: Meta announced two in-house AI smart glasses, the Adventurer and Fury, starting at $299 and dropping the Ray-Ban branding for the first time. The price undercuts the latest $379 Ray-Ban Meta models and includes a $399 Starfire model made with Kylie Jenner, with manufacturing still handled by EssilorLuxottica.

title: Sail raises $80M to make AI agents cheaper to run
blurb: Sail Research raised $80M across seed and Series A rounds at a $450M valuation, with Sequoia leading the seed and Kleiner Perkins leading the Series A. Founded by ex-Apple and ex-NVIDIA engineers, the startup rebuilt its inference stack for throughput rather than latency, claiming up to 10 times lower cost per token for agents that burn billions of tokens on a single task.

title: Coval raises $28M to stress-test AI voice agents
