blurb: Coval raised $28M in a Series A led by Norwest to run simulated stress tests on AI voice agents before they reach live callers, bringing total funding to $31M since its 2024 launch. Founded by former Waymo evaluation engineer Brooke Hopkins, the platform runs tens of millions of tests probing for accents, interruptions, and off-script requests, and counts Zoom and Deepgram among its 60-plus customers.

title: Nvidia Wants to Make Humanoid AI Robots Safer Around Humans
blurb: Nvidia is developing edge AI that lets humanoid robots recognize danger and react instantly to work safely around humans.

title: Cerebras On Track for Record Two-Day Loss as Outlook Disappoints
blurb: Cerebras Systems shares tumbled Thursday and were on track for a record two-session loss after the chipmaker issued a disappointing annual sales outlook. The selloff marks a sharp reversal for the AI accelerator maker, whose specialized wafer-scale chips have positioned it as a challenger to NVIDIA in high-speed inference workloads.

title: Alphabet Shares Fall After Report on Further AI Talent Departures
blurb: Alphabet shares fell after a report of additional AI talent departures, deepening a selloff that began last week with the exit of several AI architects. The losses follow a string of high-profile researcher moves across the industry, raising questions about Google's ability to retain key technical staff amid intensifying competition for AI expertise.

title: Netris raises $15M Series A from a16z to automate the networking that slows down GPU clouds
blurb: Netris raised $15M in a Series A led by Andreessen Horowitz to automate the networking layer that connects GPUs inside neocloud data centers. The platform runs on network switches to handle setup, abstraction, and multi-tenant isolation, and is live across 35-plus GPU clusters at customers including Lightning AI, HPE, and TensorWave following 800% annual recurring revenue growth.

title: Applied Materials unveils more advanced chipmaking gear for 3D stacking architectures
blurb: Applied Materials introduced new chip fabrication systems spanning advanced packaging, process control, and DRAM manufacturing to help customers build the 3D-stacked architectures that next-generation AI processors require. The lineup targets the "memory wall," including the Opta Quad CMP platform for surface planarity and the Nokota Vmax 2 ECD system for the high-precision copper plating that connects stacked DRAM through microscopic through-silicon vias.

title: Exclusive: LucidLink launches MCP server to give AI agents shared access to distributed files
blurb: LucidLink released a public beta of its Model Context Protocol server, exposing its distributed streaming file system so AI agents can read and write shared files across clouds, on-premises systems, and edge environments. The server connects any MCP-compatible agent or orchestrator, including Claude, OpenAI's Agents SDK, LangChain, LlamaIndex, and CrewAI, to a LucidLink filespace that gives multi-agent systems persistent, writable shared state without repeatedly copying data.

title: Micron reveals AI companies are spending billions to lock up its memory years in advance
blurb: Micron disclosed 16 strategic supply agreements backed by roughly $22 billion in customer deposits and commitments, locking up DRAM and NAND deliveries that run as far out as 2030. The deals cover about 20% of expected DRAM shipments and a third of NAND, and could generate around $100 billion in future contracted obligations as AI demand for high-bandwidth memory tightens supply.

title: The Unbearable Cheapness of Open Weight Models
blurb: Open weight models like DeepSeek V4 now run at roughly 1/50th the per-token cost of frontier APIs from Anthropic and OpenAI, and the author argues those labs may be too cost-bound to ever close that gap. Rather than cut prices 20-50x, they appear to be manufacturing scarcity through "frontier" gating and luxury-style branding, and the author worries they will lean on China fears to push regulatory bans on open weight models instead of competing.

title: AI agent identification service gets official nod from open source giant
blurb: The Linux Foundation launched the Agent Name Service (ANS), an identity framework backed by Cisco, Cloudflare, and Salesforce that builds on existing DNS infrastructure to identify, verify, and locate AI agents across the web. Originally developed by GoDaddy, ANS ties agentic identity to DNS and folds in legal entity identifiers and decentralized identifiers, letting organizations verify an agent's permissions and operational history through a single model.

title: OpenAI, Anthropic and Amazon are funding RAISE US
blurb: OpenAI, Anthropic, Amazon, and Microsoft are funding RAISE US, a nonprofit led by former commerce secretary Gina Raimondo that retrains American workers for AI-driven disruption. The group has raised over $500M toward a $1B target, with more than two dozen backers including Bank of America, IBM, AMD, and the Rockefeller Foundation.

title: Anthropic hires French telecom group Orange's Chief AI Officer Steve Jarrett to help Anthropic "better understand and adapt" its products to Europe and Africa (Gianluca Lo Nostro/Reuters)
blurb: Anthropic hired Steve Jarrett, Chief AI Officer at French telecom group Orange, to help the company adapt its products for Europe and Africa. The move signals a push to deepen Anthropic's presence in regional markets as it competes with OpenAI and Google for international enterprise and government customers.

title: How KRAFTON Built PUBG Ally, a Co-Playable Character Powered by NVIDIA ACE
blurb: KRAFTON built PUBG Ally, an AI teammate for PUBG: BATTLEGROUNDS that runs on NVIDIA ACE using automatic speech recognition, an on-device 2B-parameter language model, and text-to-speech to interpret player voice and game context in real time. The team frames it as a co-playable character that cooperates, adapts to combat and looting situations, and remembers across sessions, entering public beta in Arcade Mode from June 17 to June 30.

title: Medical students are using popular research tool to pump out misleading studies
blurb: Medical students are using a popular AI-powered research platform to rapidly generate large volumes of low-quality observational studies, often based on the same public datasets. Critics warn that the flood of formulaic, sometimes misleading papers is straining peer review and inflating publication records without adding real scientific value.

title: ISTE 2026: Supporting teaching and learning with connected AI tools
blurb: Google introduced Study notebooks in Gemini and a Classroom app, plus no-cost practice ACT and GRE tests via The Princeton Review.

title: OpenAI and Anthropic: Will the cybersec arms race break the glass on the AI giants?
blurb: OpenAI expanded its Daybreak cybersecurity program this week, adding Cato Networks and IBM alongside existing partners Cisco, Palo Alto Networks, and Fortinet to deepen its hold on the network security ecosystem. The moves escalate an arms race with Anthropic, whose Claude Mythos model and Cisco-and-Nvidia-backed Project Glasswing task force have drawn both a Trump endorsement and continued export bans.

title: TrueFoundry acquires MLOps pioneer Seldon AI to accelerate enterprise agentic AI
blurb: TrueFoundry acquired open-source MLOps platform Seldon AI, whose Kubernetes-native inference foundation now powers TrueFoundry's control plane for deploying and governing agentic AI.

title: Anthropic accuses Alibaba of campaign to 'brazenly' and 'illicitly' extract AI capabilities
blurb: Anthropic told the Senate Banking Committee that Alibaba ran the largest known distillation attack against its models to date, using roughly 25,000 fraudulent accounts to pull 28.8 million exchanges between April 22 and June 5. Distillation lets a weaker model train on a stronger one's outputs, so the cost of catching up keeps falling for whoever can scrape enough responses. For founders building on frontier APIs, the takeaway is that your model's outputs are now a contested asset, and the fight over who can copy them is moving from blog posts to Congress and export controls.

title: Apple says "the rapid expansion of AI data centers has created an extraordinary surge in demand for memory and storage"; the MacBook Neo rises from $599 to $699 (Mark Gurman/Bloomberg)
blurb: Apple warned that the rapid buildout of AI data centers has created an extraordinary surge in demand for memory and storage, and it just raised the entry MacBook Neo from $599 to $699. The price bump is the first visible sign that hyperscaler compute spending is bidding up the same DRAM and NAND that consumer hardware depends on. For founders shipping physical products or budgeting device fleets, the lesson is that AI capex is no longer a line item confined to the cloud, it now shows up at the checkout.

title: Anthropic claims that China's Alibaba used 25,000 fake accounts and 28.8 million exchanges to illicitly 'distill' its Claude model — violations occurred from April to June 2026
blurb: Anthropic told the U.S. Senate that operators linked to Alibaba ran 25,000 fake accounts and 28.8 million Claude exchanges to distill its models between April and June 2026. It is the same playbook the lab previously pinned on DeepSeek, Moonshot, and MiniMax, and the scale here is nearly double. The fight is now about who captures the returns on frontier R&D, because distillation lets a rival approximate a leading model at a fraction of the cost and time, and Chinese labs are closing the gap fast while enterprises shift cheaper workloads to their open-source models.

title: European Commission lines up Amazon and Microsoft for cloud gatekeeper status
blurb: The European Commission is preparing to designate Amazon and Microsoft as cloud gatekeepers, the same Digital Markets Act status already pinning down Apple, Google, and Meta. Gatekeeper rules force interoperability, ban self-preferencing, and carry fines up to 10% of global revenue, so the move would reach directly into how AWS and Azure bundle services and lock in customers. For any founder building on European cloud infrastructure, the likely result is more portability and fewer egress traps, but also a slower, more cautious pace from the two vendors most startups depend on.

title: Apple Raises Prices on Some Products by $500
blurb: Apple is raising prices on Macs, iPads, home devices, and the Vision Pro by up to $500 to offset a memory and storage chip shortage, while leaving the iPhone, Apple Watch, and AirPods untouched. The selective hikes signal that component costs, not demand, are now setting hardware margins. For any founder shipping physical products or buying compute, it is a reminder that the memory shortage is bleeding into end prices across the supply chain.

title: Salesforce unveils AI Help Agent with pay-per-resolution pricing
blurb: Salesforce released Agentforce Help Agent, a pre-packaged customer service agent that organizations connect to their knowledge base, with a pay-per-resolution model that charges roughly $2 only when the agent closes an issue from start to finish, no human escalation or abandonment. The shift matters because it ties vendor revenue to outcomes rather than seats, the same usage- and outcome-based pricing spreading across AI software. Salesforce is betting it can stand behind that promise after its own Help portal resolved 70% of 4.3 million inquiries.

title: The engineer who built Swift and LLVM sold his AI startup to Qualcomm for $4B
blurb: Qualcomm is acquiring Modular, the AI infrastructure startup co-founded by Chris Lattner, in an all-stock deal worth about $3.92B, doubling its valuation from $1.6B just nine months ago. Modular's pitch is hardware-agnostic AI software that lets developers write an app once and run it across Nvidia, AMD, Apple, and Qualcomm chips without rewriting their stack, and it already counts Oracle, Amazon, Nvidia, and AMD as customers. The target is the CUDA software lock-in that keeps Nvidia almost impossible to displace, and Qualcomm just bought the most credible attempt to break it.

title: OpenAI unveils first chip as part of Broadcom deal in effort to 'build the full stack'
blurb: OpenAI introduced Jalapeño, its first custom AI chip, built with Broadcom and aimed at inference, the compute that serves ChatGPT to users. The move is one piece of a wider scramble for silicon, with OpenAI also striking deals with AWS, AMD, and Cerebras because, in Greg Brockman's words, it "cannot get compute fast enough." The signal for founders is that the frontier labs are pushing down into hardware to own the full stack, and the compute crunch they're racing to solve isn't easing through 2028.

title: Sources: Google expands the scope of its months-old AI coding strike team to "midtraining" to try to catch up with Anthropic, after major executive departures (Erin Woo/The Information)
blurb: Google expanded its months-old AI coding strike team into "midtraining" to close the gap with Anthropic, following a string of senior executive departures. The move signals that catching Claude on code now means intervening deeper in the model pipeline, not just fine-tuning at the end. For founders building on these labs, the takeaway is that the coding-model race is far from settled, and even Google is reorganizing internally to keep pace with the current leader.

title: Unconventional AI releases its first model, built on an oscillator architecture its founder says could cut power use by a factor of a thousand
blurb: Unconventional AI released Un-0, an image generation model matching diffusion quality on simulated oscillator hardware that doesn't exist yet.

title: Trump admin proposes axing brake-pedal requirement for AVs in a boost for Tesla
blurb: The Trump DOT proposed letting companies skip brake pedals and other manual controls on vehicles built to be driven exclusively by automated systems, removing a barrier that previously forced AV makers to seek capped federal exemptions. The change directly benefits Tesla's Cybercab and Zoox, which are designing cars with no steering wheel or pedals, and could put fully driverless vehicles on roads faster once approved. The public has 30 days to comment, and it extends a deregulation push that started under Biden, who finalized a rule allowing AVs without steering wheels.

title: Chinese A.I. Models Gain Ground on Anthropic and OpenAI
blurb: Chinese AI models are closing the gap with the frontier labs, and price is the lever. Z.ai's latest release performs almost as well as the best from Anthropic and OpenAI at a fraction of the cost, enough that Silicon Valley engineers are now adopting it directly. The pressure point for US labs isn't capability anymore but the premium they can charge, and any startup building on frontier APIs now has a credible, far cheaper alternative one swap away.

title: Databricks’ former AI chief thinks he can cut AI’s power bill by 1,000x
blurb: Unconventional AI, led by former Databricks AI chief Naveen Rao, released Un-0, an image-generation model that matches state-of-the-art diffusion quality while running on a simulation of a new oscillator-based chip architecture Rao claims can cut inference power use by up to 1,000x. The company is still building the actual chips, with schematics promised soon and a full inference stack to follow. If power is the hard ceiling on AI as Rao argues, a sub-50-person startup rethinking computing from the substrate up is one of the few bets sized to the problem.

title: AI Revenue Starts to Exceed a Key Infrastructure Cost But Not By Much
blurb: AI revenue at the biggest cloud players has finally crossed $25B, edging past the depreciation on the infrastructure built to generate it, but the margin is thin. For Meta, Alphabet, and Microsoft the numbers now technically work, while Google and Amazon still need sustained premium pricing and strong user growth to recoup what they've spent. The takeaway is that the capex bet is no longer underwater, but it isn't paying off either, and the gap between spending and return stays narrow enough that any slip in demand flips the math back to red.

title: Apple stock drops 5% on MacBook and iPad price hikes due to memory crunch
blurb: Apple raised prices on MacBooks and iPads for the first time to pass component costs on to consumers, and the stock fell more than 5%, its worst day since April 2025. Memory and storage prices have quadrupled in three quarters as suppliers divert production to the high-bandwidth memory feeding AI data centers, with Micron's gross margin jumping from 39% to 84.9%. The AI compute boom is now showing up on consumer hardware sticker prices, and Cook says more increases are likely because the component squeeze is far from over.

title: Health unicorn Alan raises €480m Series G round led by Prosus
blurb: Alan raised €480m in a Series G led by Prosus, valuing the French digital insurer at €5.5bn. The company crossed €800m in ARR in Q1 2026, up 53% year-on-year, while serving more than 1.1m people and staying profitable in its home market of France. The pull is its AI-native model, which lets Alan scale care and insurance with limited headcount growth, turning a stagnant sector into one of the clearest places for AI to compound margins as it expands across Europe.

title: N26 hits profitability following leadership shakeup
blurb: N26 booked its first full year of net profitability in 2025, posting €1.6m net income against a €42m loss the year before, with revenue up 13% to €501.6m. The turnaround landed in the same year investors pushed out both cofounders over regulatory sanctions, replacing them with new CEO Mike Dargan and a stack of BaFin restrictions still capping growth in markets like the Netherlands. The lesson for founders building in regulated categories is that compliance debt eventually shows up on the income statement, and it can cost you the company you started.

title: General Intuition, which trains AI agents in spatial reasoning via gameplay footage, raised $320M led by Khosla at a $2.3B valuation, for $454M in total funding (Rebecca Bellan/TechCrunch)
blurb: General Intuition raised $320M led by Khosla at a $2.3B valuation, bringing total funding to $454M to train AI agents on spatial reasoning using gameplay footage. The bet is that video games are a cheaper, richer source of physical-world reasoning data than the real-world capture most robotics and agent labs rely on. Spatial reasoning remains one of the weakest links in current models, and whoever supplies that capability cheaply becomes infrastructure for the robotics and agent companies building on top.

title: Airwallex worth $16 billion after $460 million Series H
blurb: Airwallex raised $460M in a Series H led by Addition, lifting the Melbourne-founded fintech's valuation 37% to $16B and pushing annualized revenue to $1.3B, up 74% on the year. More than 90% of that revenue now comes from customers using more than one product, the kind of multi-product lock-in that funds an expansion into autonomous finance and agentic commerce. The round closed despite an AUSTRAC investigation into anti-money-laundering compliance, a reminder that regulatory exposure is no longer disqualifying when the underlying infrastructure and growth are strong enough.

title: Apple Shares Sink After Sweeping Price Hikes Hit iPads and Macs
blurb: Apple raised prices across its entire Mac, iPad, home device, and Vision Pro lineup to offset an unprecedented memory chip shortage, and shares fell on the news. The increases mark a rare move for a company that usually holds pricing steady and absorbs component swings through scale and supplier leverage. The signal for hardware founders is that memory and storage costs have climbed far enough that even Apple can't eat them, so anyone shipping physical product should expect tighter margins or higher prices through the rest of the year.

title: Apple raises Mac and iPad prices, spares iPhone for now
blurb: Apple raised prices across its Mac and iPad lineups, blaming a worldwide memory shortage driven by the AI data center buildout, with the MacBook Air jumping from $1,099 to $1,299 and the iPad Air from $599 to $749. The iPhone is spared for now because its constraint is the main processor, not memory, but Tim Cook has called further hikes "unavoidable" and a bump could land later this year. DRAM prices are up 50% and NAND over 90% quarter-over-quarter, so any hardware-dependent business pricing against AI-era component costs should expect the squeeze to spread.

title: Sail, whose software optimizes how AI models run on existing chips, emerges from stealth with $80M in seed and Series A led by Kleiner at a $450M valuation (Lily Mae Lazarus/Fortune)
blurb: Sail emerged from stealth with $80M in combined seed and Series A led by Kleiner Perkins at a $450M valuation. Its software squeezes more performance out of AI models on the chips companies already own, rather than chasing scarce new hardware. That points at a quieter shift in the AI buildout: with GPU supply tight and compute capex ballooning, the cheapest path to more capacity is increasingly software that wrings more out of the silicon already racked, not another order with Nvidia.

title: EU says AWS and Microsoft Azure should be treated as DMA gatekeepers
blurb: The European Commission told Amazon and Microsoft it considers AWS and Azure gatekeepers under the DMA, even though the cloud services don't clearly meet the law's numeric thresholds. The Commission's reasoning is that the two largest cloud providers in the EU sit on entrenched user bases protected by high switching costs and lock-in, which is exactly the leverage the DMA exists to curb. If the preliminary view holds, Amazon and Microsoft get six months to comply with interoperability and fair-access rules, and any founder building on top of either platform inherits a more portable, less captive cloud.

title: DeepSeek Plans to Double Staff Across All Departments
blurb: DeepSeek plans to at least double headcount across every department, fresh off agreements for a blockbuster funding round. The hiring push is the clearest sign yet that the Chinese lab intends to compete head-on with OpenAI and Anthropic, not just its domestic rivals. The race for frontier AI is no longer a Silicon Valley story, and the capital and talent to contest it are now pooling on both sides of the Pacific.

title: OpenAI, Anthropic and SpaceX Are Shaking Up VC Fundraising
blurb: A handful of giants now drives the entire VC fundraising market. OpenAI, Anthropic, and SpaceX have become large enough that the prospect of outsized gains lets firms raise record amounts of capital just to buy into them. The result is a barbell: money pools into a few late-stage names while the long tail of startups competes for what's left, which reshapes who gets funded and on what terms.

title: Micron is up 13% after blockbuster earnings, but has pulled back from highs
blurb: Micron's revenue more than quadrupled to $41.46B last quarter as AI data centers hoover up memory chips, beating estimates by over $5B, and the company is guiding to roughly $50B this quarter. The more durable signal is that it has locked in 16 long-term contracts worth $22B in committed spend, with about 40% of revenue now carrying a minimum price over three to five years. That structure limits the margin downside if the cycle turns, which is the part that should interest anyone betting on memory pricing holding through 2027.

title: N26 hits first full-year profit, amid challenging 2025
blurb: N26 posted its first full-year profit, €1.6M in net income for 2025 against a €42M loss the year before, on €501.6M in revenue. The turn came from the unglamorous levers: card transaction volumes, subscription growth that pushed fee income up 21%, and a 17% cut to direct costs. It arrives amid a founder exit and fresh German regulatory sanctions, with new CEO Mike Dargan now steering 5.6M paying customers toward becoming primary-bank relationships, the deposits that make a challenger bank durable rather than just large.

title: French health insurance outfit Alan agrees €480M funding round
blurb: Alan raised €480M in a Series G at a €5.5bn valuation, led by new investor Prosus and coming just months after a €100M round at €5bn. The Paris-based health insurer crossed €800M in ARR in Q1 2026 with 1.1 million customers across France, Spain, Belgium, and Canada. The pace tells the story: European insurtech that pairs coverage with care navigation and AI health tooling is now compounding fast enough to raise twice in a single year, and Alan plans to spend the money on new countries and acquisitions.

title: Amazon, Microsoft’s Cloud Services Should Fall Under DMA, EU Says
blurb: The EU wants to designate Amazon and Microsoft as DMA gatekeepers for their cloud services, subjecting AWS and Azure to the same competition rules already binding their search and app-store businesses. The companies would have to prove they aren't tilting the field against rivals, which could mean curbing egress fees, licensing lock-in, and bundled discounts that keep customers from switching. For founders building on a single cloud, the practical upside is cheaper exit and more leverage at renewal, but the rules would land first in Europe and likely take years to bite.

title: Agility, Maker of Humanlike Robots, to Go Public in $2.5 Billion SPAC Deal
blurb: Agility Robotics, maker of the Digit humanoid robot already working in Amazon warehouses, agreed to go public through a SPAC deal valuing it at $2.5B. The listing makes Agility one of the first humanoid-robot companies to reach public markets, putting hard revenue and deployment numbers in front of investors who have so far funded the category on promise alone. Public scrutiny will test whether warehouse and manufacturing pilots are turning into durable contracts, and the valuation sets a reference point every other humanoid startup raising private money will now be measured against.

title: Here are all of the Apple price hikes
blurb: Apple raised prices across most of its hardware lineup today, from a $30 bump on the HomePod mini to as much as $2,800 on a maxed-out MacBook Pro, blaming the surge in memory and storage costs as AI companies stockpile RAM and SSDs for data centers. Tim Cook had said the company tried to shield customers from the increases, but Apple has now joined Nintendo, Sony, and Microsoft in passing them through. The AI buildout is no longer just a cloud-capex story; it is showing up on the price tag of every consumer device that ships with memory.

title: General Intuition’s $2.3B bet that video games can train AI agents for the real world
blurb: General Intuition raised $320M at a $2.3B valuation to train AI agents on hundreds of millions of hours of gameplay clips, betting that the same model can move from games to simulation to physical robots. The differentiator is the action labels embedded in those clips, the exact buttons players pressed and when, which most competitors try to infer from video alone. The startup says it fine-tuned a model for a quadruped robot with just eight minutes of real-world data, the bet being that spatial-temporal reasoning learned in games generalizes to embodiment.

title: The AI era requires a different kind of experimentation.
blurb: Your 2022 experimentation playbook optimized for minor tweaks, slow rollouts, and two-week wins, mostly pulling forward revenue that would have arrived anyway rather than driving step-function change. The author argues AI has collapsed the build cost that justified that caution, so the smart play is to run fewer cosmetic tests and aim experiments at foundational complexity and monetization, the bets that were too slow to attempt before. Some things are now cheap enough to just ship, which means the real skill is deciding what no longer needs an A/B test at all.

title: Amazon ups India bet with fresh $13B AI infrastructure investment
blurb: Amazon committed another $13B to expand AWS data center capacity in India through 2030, pushing its total India commitments to $48 billion. The move follows Microsoft's $17.5B and Google's $15B pledges, plus data center money from AirTrunk, CPP Investments, Reliance, and Adani, all betting India becomes a core hub for AI compute. New Delhi is fueling the race with tax exemptions for foreign cloud providers running overseas workloads from Indian data centers, which means the next wave of cheap, scalable inference capacity is increasingly being built outside the usual US and European corridors.

title: Sail raises $80M to make AI agents cheaper to run
blurb: Sail Research raised $80M across seed and Series A at a $450M valuation, with Sequoia leading the seed and Kleiner Perkins the Series A, to rebuild AI inference for agents rather than people. The bet is that today's stack optimizes for a human waiting on one fast reply, while an agent runs for hours and burns billions of tokens, caring about throughput and cost instead. Sail claims up to 10 times lower cost per token, plus sandboxes that bill only for the time an agent is actually working.

title: Small satellite operators confront a bottleneck to space access
blurb: Small satellite makers built their business plans on cheap, frequent SpaceX rideshares, and that assumption is now breaking. At least nine SpaceX customers say Transporter reservations are closed beyond late 2028, with available slots potentially halved while manufacturers keep producing satellites faster than launch capacity grows. The squeeze is already visible: SEOPS sold out a 2028 Falcon 9 in three weeks and stacked roughly 30 customers on a waitlist. For founders building on the $8,000-per-kilogram rideshare math, the supply of cheap orbits can no longer be taken for granted.

title: Runpod raises $100M to build the leading cloud platform for AI developers
blurb: Runpod raised $100M in Series A funding led by Summit Partners at a $1B valuation, betting that AI builders want one place to experiment, train, fine-tune, and deploy rather than the inference-only clouds the market narrowed to. The platform has pulled in more than one million developers and served over 20 billion inference requests, with 80% of developers who deploy coming back to build more. The bet is that as model work moves from inference back toward full-lifecycle development, the infrastructure that wins is the one builders never have to stitch together.

title: An Interview with Figma CEO Dylan Field About Design and AI
blurb: Figma's market cap has fallen below $10B, less than half the $20B Adobe offered before regulators killed that deal in 2023, after peaking near $56.3B at its 2025 IPO. The market has decided Figma is an AI loser, with generative design tools threatening to commoditize the work its canvas was built for. Field argues the opposite, framing AI as a tailwind and positioning Figma's canvas as the natural place where design and code converge, which is the bet founders watching the design-tooling stack should weigh against the consensus.

title: Taktile raises $110M to automate banking’s riskiest calls
blurb: Taktile raised $110M in a Series C led by Goldman Sachs Alternatives, pushing total funding to $184M, to put AI agents on the calls banks dread getting wrong: underwriting loans, vetting customers, and flagging financial crime. The pitch is deliberately narrow, a decision layer that wraps frontier models in hard rules and human oversight rather than another chatbot. Taktile says customers hit 95% automation in B2B underwriting and cut anti-money-laundering false alarms 75%, with one large insurer projecting over $90M in claims-processing savings. The signal for founders: in regulated work, the moat is the trust layer, not the model.

title: SpaceX Credit Derivatives Start to Trade After Debut Bond Sale
blurb: SpaceX credit-default swaps have begun actively trading after the company sold its first high-grade bonds this week, giving investors a way to hedge or speculate on its creditworthiness. A live CDS market means SpaceX is now priced as a credit, not just a venture valuation, with a daily read on default risk set by traders rather than late-stage rounds. For founders, it marks how far the largest private companies have drifted into public-market machinery while staying off the exchanges.

title: Cursor Deal Puts US On Track For Record Startup M&A Year
blurb: US startup M&A is on pace for a record year, with acquirers spending at least $119.8 billion on venture-backed companies in 2026, already tracking past last year's high. About half of that is a single deal: SpaceX's $60 billion purchase of Cursor and parent Anysphere, the largest startup acquisition ever, nearly double Google's $32 billion Wiz buy. Strip out that one transaction and the picture is steadier than the headline suggests, with the next nine biggest deals ranging from $2 billion to $7 billion and biotech, led by Eli Lilly, accounting for half of them.

title: Samsung Joins With US Battery Startup Seeking Pentagon Cash
blurb: Samsung SDI is putting $20M and engineering support into Forge Nano, a US battery startup building cells for defense and aerospace at a new North Carolina plant. The deal pairs a Korean manufacturing giant with a startup chasing Pentagon money, a route more hardware founders are taking as defense budgets become a reliable source of non-dilutive capital. It also signals that domestic battery production for military use is becoming a category strategic enough to pull in foreign incumbents looking for a foothold.

title: Netris raises $15M Series A from a16z to automate the networking that slows down GPU clouds
blurb: Netris raised $15M in a Series A led by a16z to automate the networking layer inside GPU data centers, the unglamorous bottleneck that leaves neoclouds with billions in GPUs sitting idle for months. The company reports 800% annual recurring revenue growth and more than 35 live cluster deployments, including Lightning AI, HPE, and TensorWave. While the AI infrastructure story fixates on chips and power, the harder constraint is the network fabric connecting thousands of servers, where a single misconfiguration can take a cluster down or leak one tenant's data to another.

title: Coval raises $28M to stress-test AI voice agents
blurb: Coval raised $28M in a Series A led by Norwest to stress-test AI voice agents before they reach real callers, bringing total funding to $31M since 2024. The bet is that voice agents need the same simulation-first discipline as self-driving cars, running tens of millions of tests against accents, interruptions, background noise, and off-script callers. As more firms put voice agents in front of customers, the value shifts to proving they actually work, and Coval says its more than 60 users, including Zoom and Deepgram, cut manual QA up to 30 times.

title: Google Play updates let U.K. developers do more and pay less.
blurb: Google Play is cutting service fees for U.K. developers starting June 30, letting them run their own billing systems alongside Google's or send users to their websites for digital purchases. Developers who join programs like the revamped Games Level Up get even lower rates from September 30, and a Registered App Stores program will streamline installs for stores that meet safety criteria later this year. The changes answer CMA concerns in a market where Android already generates over £9.9B in annual revenue for U.K. developers.

title: Google will open the Play Store to outside billing on June 30
blurb: Google will open the Play Store to outside billing on June 30 in the US, UK, and Europe, and cut its commission from 30% to 10% on a developer's first $1M in annual earnings, settling its court fight with Epic. Developers can route payments to their own sites or alternative processors, with the 10% service fee separated from a 5% billing fee that only applies when Google Play handles the transaction. The cheaper economics reset the math for any Android app where in-app purchases drive revenue, though fees climb to 20-25% past the first million and the change goes worldwide by September 2027.

title: ARX Robotics and Roboneers form ARX Industries to scale unmanned ground vehicle production
blurb: Germany's ARX Robotics and Ukraine's Roboneers merged into ARX Industries, a joint venture to mass-produce the Rys Pro unmanned ground vehicle across facilities in both countries. The plan is thousands of units in year one, scaling to tens of thousands annually, feeding Ukraine's target of 50,000 UGVs in 2026 for casualty evacuation, logistics, and combat roles. The edge is a battlefield-to-factory feedback loop, with each production batch reflecting lessons from active deployment, and government backing on both sides marks it as a bet on European defense sovereignty.

title: Hey Nico, you didn't vibe code your data room but stole it from Papermark
blurb: Papermark's founder publicly accused another YC founder of stealing its open-source and enterprise-licensed code to build a competing data-room product, calling it copyright infringement rather than vibe coding. The open-source angle cuts both ways: publishing your code earns trust and contributions, but it also hands competitors a working blueprint and shifts the fight onto license enforcement. For founders building on permissive or dual-licensed code, the moat was never the code itself.

title: Netris raises $15M Series A from a16z to help AI neoclouds go live faster
blurb: Netris raised $15M in a Series A led by a16z to automate the network setup that slows neocloud operators down. Standing up a GPU data center for AI training and inference can take months of configuring switches, storage, and multi-tenancy, and every day of delay means expensive GPUs sitting idle. Netris sells hardware-accelerated software that runs on the switches themselves, handling configuration, isolation, and multi-tenancy that hyperscalers built in-house with armies of engineers. Nvidia was impressed enough to start recommending the eight-year-old company to its own customers.

title: OpenAI, Anthropic and Amazon are funding RAISE US
blurb: OpenAI, Anthropic, Microsoft and Amazon are fierce rivals, but they have all signed checks for RAISE US, a new nonprofit retraining American workers for the AI economy. Led by former commerce secretary Gina Raimondo, the group has raised more than $500m against a $1bn target, with two dozen companies including Bank of America, IBM, and Mastercard backing it. The labs preparing to go public at trillion-dollar valuations are now paying to soften the disruption their own models create, stepping into a vacuum Washington has left open.

title: You may see new ways to pay for Android apps and subscriptions starting next week
blurb: Starting June 30, Android developers in the US, UK, and Europe can route purchases outside Google Play Billing and skip the cut.

title: Booking.com for Business Shipped a Free Expense Tool in 6 Weeks. Don’t Copy the Tool, Copy the Sequence.
blurb: Booking.com for Business shipped a free expense tool in six weeks by inverting the usual order: instead of asking what AI can do, they hunted for where customers hit friction. In B2B that matters because friction is where churn lives, the moments users stall out and leave, so the admin wrapping a trip, reconstructing a $42 receipt three weeks later, was a bigger pain than booking the trip itself. The playbook to copy isn't the expense product, it's the sequence of finding the friction and building straight at it.

title: Notion plans to shut down its Gmail client Notion Mail on September 22 and go "all in" on AI agents to run inboxes, saying 50%+ of users do not open the inbox (Zac Hall/9to5Mac)
blurb: Notion is shutting down its Gmail client Notion Mail on September 22 to go all in on AI agents that run the inbox, citing that more than half of users never open it. The bet is that founders don't want a better mail client, they want the inbox handled, and Notion would rather kill a product people ignore than keep maintaining it. If even the company that built the inbox thinks the inbox itself is the wrong surface, the email startups still shipping faster clients are building for a job that's quietly disappearing.

title: Gates Foundation backs Leyden Labs’ €40M raise: Can a nasal spray do what 80 years of flu vaccines couldn’t
blurb: Leyden Labs raised €40M to push PanFlu, a nasal spray of broadly protective antibodies, through clinical trials, with the Gates Foundation joining for the first time to widen access in low- and middle-income countries. The bet is that targeting conserved regions of influenza A and B lets one self-administered spray hold up against new strains, sidestepping the seasonal reformulation window that leaves the elderly and immunocompromised exposed. The round takes Leyden past $336M in total funding, making it one of the best-capitalized respiratory virus biotechs outside the US.

title: Parker Conrad knows which employees are worth their AI spend and says Rippling can help you, too
blurb: Rippling launched Rippling Data Cloud, a bet that the modern data stack (Fivetran, Snowflake, dbt, Tableau) collapses into the HR system that already knows your org chart. Parker Conrad's pitch is that wrapping warehouse, transform, and BI layers in a live understanding of reporting structure lets you cross-reference Anthropic usage logs, GitHub PRs, and performance ratings to see which employees actually return their AI spend. He demoed finding a worker on a $30,000-a-year Claude run rate with no matching ROI. The play positions an HR vendor to compete head-on with dedicated business intelligence tools.

title: AI Sales Start to Justify Data-Center Spending Boom, Report Says
blurb: AI revenue has reached a tipping point where the hundreds of billions tech giants are pouring into data centers may finally be economically sustainable, according to a new report from Exponential View. The spending case had rested on faith that demand would eventually catch up to capex, and the numbers are starting to back it. For founders, the read is that the infrastructure layer is consolidating around players whose AI revenue can actually carry the buildout, narrowing where the next wave of compute and capital will flow.

title: Micron's AI Pricing Power Explained
blurb: Memory chips have become a strategic asset, and Micron now holds pricing power that may have permanently reset the semiconductor industry. AI demand turned commodity DRAM into a constrained resource, handing memory makers leverage they never had through prior cycles. The counterintuitive read is that Apple could come out stronger despite paying more, because rivals without its margins and supply scale will absorb the cost increases far less gracefully.

title: British Business Bank backs 10 microfunds with £90M, with most GPs women or from ethnic minority backgrounds
blurb: The British Business Bank is backing 10 first-time venture capital managers with up to £90M, the opening checks from its £400M Investor Pathways Capital initiative. More than half the general partners are women and 43% come from ethnic minority backgrounds, including what is billed as the first Black-led VC fund in the UK and Europe. Each manager gets a £10M to £20M cornerstone commitment to write pre-seed and seed checks across AI, deep tech, climate, defence, and consumer, opening the LP base to managers institutional capital has historically skipped.

title: Data Center Surge Brings Risk for States and Munis, Moody’s Says
blurb: Moody's warns that the data center building boom is now a credit risk for the states and municipalities racing to host it. The projects arrive with enormous power and water demands that strain local utilities, push up infrastructure costs, and concentrate revenue in a single volatile tenant. For founders chasing the AI infrastructure trade, the constraint is shifting from chips to the grid and the balance sheets of the governments that have to underwrite it.

title: Germany’s Merck to Boost Lab-Tools Business With $11 Billion Bio-Techne Deal
blurb: Germany’s Merck agreed to buy Bio-Techne for $11B, its largest acquisition in more than a decade and the first major bet from new CEO Kai Beckmann to expand its life-science lab-tools business. The deal pushes Merck deeper into reagents and instruments at a moment when biotech research budgets are tightening and consolidation is picking up. For founders selling into pharma and lab workflows, the buyers are getting bigger and the procurement decisions are concentrating into fewer, larger platforms.

title: Interesting Paper Exploring Prompt Injection
blurb: A new paper argues that LLMs fall for prompt injection because role tags are a formatting convention that never becomes a real boundary in the model's internal representations, with the model leaning on the style of text in each block rather than the tags themselves. The authors frame injection as role confusion and conclude that without genuine role perception, defenses stay a whack-a-mole game vulnerable to attacks that subtly shift model state through innocuous-looking text.

title: DeepReinforce releases Ornith-1.0 open-source coding models
blurb: DeepReinforce open-sourced Ornith-1.0, a family of agentic coding models spanning a 9B Dense build for edge use up to a 397B MoE flagship, all trained on Gemma 4 and Qwen 3.5 foundations. Instead of relying on human-designed harnesses, each RL step has the model author its own task-specific scaffold alongside the solution, with the 397B reaching 77.5 on Terminal-Bench 2.1 and 82.4 on SWE-Bench Verified.

title: How we built saga rollbacks for Cloudflare Workflows
blurb: Cloudflare shipped saga rollbacks for Workflows, letting developers declare compensation logic inside each step.do() rather than tracking what succeeded and what to undo in separate orchestration code outside the steps. Each rollback runs as its own durable step that must be idempotent and continue even if a prior compensation fails, so a partially completed multi-step transaction can semantically reverse committed operations like a debit at one bank after a downstream credit fails.

title: Dolphin Emulator Progress Release 2606
blurb: The Dolphin team published its 2606 progress report, documenting the GameCube and Wii emulator changes merged since the last release, spanning the JIT recompiler, graphics backends, and hardware accuracy fixes. The write-up walks through each notable pull request with the specific game compatibility regressions or speedups it produced, the standard format the project uses to surface low-level emulation work that rarely gets its own changelog entry.

title: Apple takes over Swift Package Index, vows to remove  GitHub dependency
blurb: Apple took over stewardship of the Swift Package Index and pledged to remove its dependency on GitHub, which currently backs the registry's package metadata and hosting. The move gives Apple direct control over the canonical index Swift developers rely on for dependency resolution, raising questions about portability for projects hosted outside GitHub.

title: New Linux Foundation project aims to bring DNS-style trust to AI agents
blurb: The Linux Foundation announced the Agent Name Service, a framework built on DNS that gives AI agents verifiable identity, ownership, and capability records. Enterprises publish agent identities through domains they already control, letting other systems verify who an agent represents and what permissions it holds before interacting, with no reliance on a proprietary registry or centralized control.

title: Show HN: I made Google Trends for Hacker News by indexing 18 years of comments
blurb: Hacker News Trends indexes 18 years of Hacker News in a Google Trends-style interface, charting how often any topic, tool, or person appears across 45M posts and comments via live date-histograms on Upstash Redis Search. Overlay multiple terms to compare traction over time, then drop below the chart to read the actual stories and comments behind each line, filterable by term or author.

title: Zero-Downtime Deployments with Docker Compose – No Kubernetes Required
blurb: StatusDude runs zero-downtime production deploys for thousands of monitoring checks per minute using only Docker Compose and HAProxy, no Kubernetes. The post documents why Traefik failed during rolling deploys, with duplicate-label 404s, a scale-down race that kept routing 502s to shutting-down containers, and no retry on a different backend, then how HAProxy fixed it.

title: Microsoft updates Visual Studio Code with chat cost tracking and multi-agent chats
blurb: VS Code 1.126 adds session-level chat cost tracking, surfacing the total credit spend of a conversation rather than per-turn costs so you can find and trim expensive sessions. The release also lets a Copilot agent-host session hold multiple side-by-side chats with independent histories, and switches untrusted folders to open automatically in Restricted Mode behind a trust-management banner.

title: Markdy: Like Mermaid Diagrams, but for Motion
blurb: Markdy is a text-based DSL for animations, letting you define scenes, actors, and timelines in a .markdy file while a pure-TypeScript parser and the Web Animations API handle rendering. The structured syntax is designed for LLMs to generate and iterate on, ships as separate core and renderer packages with zero runtime deps, and includes an Astro island component.

title: Deno 2.9
blurb: Deno 2.9 ships deno desktop, an experimental command that wraps a script or auto-detected web framework project (Next.js, Astro, SvelteKit, and others) into a single native binary with the UI in a webview and logic in Deno, no Electron or Tauri toolchain required. The release also lets deno install read npm, pnpm, yarn, and Bun lockfiles directly, and adds CSS module imports, a stronger test runner, faster startup, and Node.js 26 compatibility.

title: Scaling AI Inference Across Multiple GPUs Using NVIDIA TensorRT with Multi-Device Inference Support
blurb: TensorRT 11.0 adds multi-device inference support, bringing native multi-GPU inference to the TensorRT runtime while preserving kernel fusions, memory planning, and quantization across devices. The feature integrates directly with NCCL for transport optimization across NVLink, NVSwitch, PCIe, and InfiniBand, and supports tensor and context parallelism so layer weights that exceed a single GPU's memory can be sharded across multiple devices.

title: New MCP specification kills old risks but opens fresh attack surfaces, Akamai finds
blurb: Akamai's analysis of the MCP 2026-07-28 specification finds the rewrite removes protocol-managed sessions and the Mcp-Session-Id header, restricts server-initiated prompts, and mandates OAuth 2.1 with PKCE, closing several session-hijacking and authentication vectors. The shift to a stateless model pushes those guarantees onto developers, who must now use unpredictable tracking IDs and validate client-returned state objects or risk attackers hijacking another user's active workflow.

title: Zig's new bitCast semantics and LLVM back end improvements
blurb: Zig's latest main-branch changes redefine `@bitCast` as a pure value operation rather than memory reinterpretation, and rework the LLVM backend to store arbitrary bit-width integers in zero- or sign-extended ABI-sized slots instead of LLVM's poorly-tested bit-int types. The new lowering matches how Clang emits C's `_BitInt(N)`, fixing missed optimizations and miscompilations the old path triggered.

title: Optimizing cloud economics with linear elastic caching
blurb: Google researchers introduced linear elastic caching, a CIDR paper that treats cache memory as a variable cost integrated over time rather than a fixed pre-allocated pool, framing each eviction as a ski-rental problem solved with lightweight ML. Instead of sizing a cache for peak demand and paying for idle RAM, the system dynamically resizes against real-time workloads to minimize total cost. The pitch matters where serverless memory runs up to $3 per GiB per day.

title: Adobe Acquires AI Upscaling Specialists Topaz Labs
blurb: Adobe acquired Topaz Labs, the Dallas-based maker of AI upscaling, denoising, and sharpening tools Gigapixel and Topaz Photo, in a deal expected to close in the second half of 2026. Adobe will integrate Topaz's enhancement models across Photoshop, Lightroom, and Firefly, sharpening its hand against Canva and DaVinci Resolve while keeping Topaz's standalone apps and CEO Eric Yang in place.

title: Markdy: Like Mermaid Diagrams, but for Motion
blurb: Markdy is a text-based DSL for motion graphics that lets you define scenes, actors, and timelines in a `.markdy` file, with a pure-TypeScript parser rendering through the Web Animations API and CSS transforms rather than Canvas or GSAP. The structured syntax is designed for LLMs to generate and validate, and ships as tree-shakeable npm packages plus an Astro island component for diffable, version-controllable animation.

title: An Interview with Figma CEO Dylan Field About Design and AI
blurb: Figma CEO Dylan Field used a Stratechery interview to push back on the market narrative that the company is an AI loser, arguing that Canvas is the natural meeting point of design and AI and a tailwind rather than a headwind. The framing matters because Figma's market cap has fallen below $10B since its 2025 IPO peak of $56.3B, less than half of Adobe's abandoned 2022 acquisition offer.

title: Adobe acquires image and video enhancement tool maker Topaz Labs
blurb: Adobe acquired Topaz Labs, the two-decade-old maker of AI image and video enhancement models including Astra for video upscaling and Wonder for image retouching, and will fold its tools into Firefly and Creative Cloud. The deal adds on-device optimization of large AI models to Adobe's stack as it defends its editing turf against Canva and Blackmagic's DaVinci Resolve.

title: How two Star Trek fans made three films using Unreal Engine and just an iPhone
blurb: Loreley Productions, a German fan studio, shoots its Star Trek films by running stripped-down Unreal Engine environments through an iPhone with Lightcraft Jetset Cine, turning the phone into a live-production viewfinder that shows a proxy of the final composite in place of a green screen. The setup lets a small team block scenes against a rough version of the virtual world, mimicking the depth and cinematography of far larger productions.

title: Interesting Paper Exploring Prompt Injection
blurb: A new paper, "Prompt Injection as Role Confusion," argues that LLMs don't actually represent role tags as security boundaries but instead learn the textual style of each role/instruction block, which is why prompt injection succeeds. The authors conclude that without genuine role perception, injection defense stays whack-a-mole, and continuous role boundaries enable attacks that subtly shift model state through innocuous-looking text at scale.

title: New Gaslight macOS Malware Uses Prompt Injection to Disrupt AI-Assisted Analysis
blurb: SentinelOne disclosed Gaslight, a Rust-based macOS implant and infostealer attributed with high confidence to North Korea-aligned actors, which embeds a prompt injection payload of fabricated system-failure messages to make LLM-assisted triage agents abort their own analysis. It persists via a LaunchAgent masquerading as "com.apple.system.services.activity," runs a Telegram bot C2 with a six-command shell, and harvests keychain, browser, and Terminal data into an exfiltrated ZIP.

title: Chrome Ad Blocker with 10M+ Installs Found with Dormant Script Injection Capability
blurb: Adblock for YouTube, a Featured Chrome extension with over 10M installs, contains a dormant script-injection path that researchers at Island found can execute arbitrary JavaScript on any site via a single server-side config change, with no extension update or store review. No malicious payload has been observed, but the capability enables page reading, data theft, and session hijacking inside authenticated apps, and the extension shares ties to related ad blockers already pulled for malware.

title: Lantronix Serial-to-IP Converter Flaw Exploited in Attacks After OT Threat Warning
blurb: CVE-2025-67038, an unauthenticated OS command injection in Lantronix EDS5000 serial-to-IP device servers that executes with root privileges, is being exploited in the wild, prompting CISA to add it to the KEV catalog on June 23 with a June 26 federal remediation deadline. Part of Forescout's BRIDGE:BREAK disclosures, the flaw gives attackers a foothold to pivot into OT and healthcare networks, so isolate exposed device servers and apply Lantronix fixes immediately.

title: STOCKSTAY Another Day: The Latest Addition to Turla’s Intelligence Gathering Apparatus
blurb: Google Threat Intelligence Group attributed STOCKSTAY, a multi-component .NET backdoor deployed since at least December 2022, to FSB-linked Turla (aka Secret Blizzard, UAC-0194), citing significant code overlap with the group's KAZUAR toolkit. The backdoor has hit Ukrainian government and military targets and entities tied to Italian foreign policy, communicating with C2 over secure WebSocket via the websocket-sharp library for ongoing espionage.

title: Cellebrite said it cut off Russia, but Russia used its tools anyway
blurb: Citizen Lab found that a Russian government investigative unit used a Cellebrite phone-hacking tool to break into the iPhone of opposition politician Andrey Pivovarov in June 2021, three months after Cellebrite publicly announced it would immediately stop selling to Russian government customers. The case shows that revoking a license or halting sales does not prevent a former customer from continuing to abuse forensic extraction tools already in their possession.

title: Bluekit phishing kit adopts browser-in-the-middle for login theft
blurb: The Bluekit phishing-as-a-service kit has swapped adversary-in-the-middle for a browser-in-the-middle technique, using the legitimate rrweb library to stream a DOM of the real login page over WebSocket and capture valid session tokens. Netcraft logged nearly 70 new hostnames in the past week; defenders should watch for input latency on login pages and pair rrweb detection with broader context, since it alone is not an indicator of compromise.

title: Russia uses Cellebrite to break into human rights activist’s phone, even after cancellation of contract
blurb: Citizen Lab found that Russian authorities used Cellebrite's UFED forensic tool to extract data from imprisoned activist Andrey Pivovarov's phone around June 2021, roughly three months after Cellebrite canceled its Russian contracts. The report notes that UFED's offline mode and legacy hardware kept working without vendor support, undermining the cancellation, and that data pulled from the device may have fed FSB-linked surveillance of other dissidents.

title: Anthropic accuses Alibaba of using 25,000 fake accounts to scrape Claude AI
blurb: Anthropic accused operators affiliated with Alibaba and its Qwen AI lab of running roughly 25,000 fraudulent accounts to extract Claude's capabilities via distillation, generating over 28.8 million exchanges between April 22 and June 5 in what it called the largest such attack against it. The company warned the campaign could accelerate China's path to its Mythos Preview model, following similar extraction efforts it earlier attributed to DeepSeek, Moonshot AI, and MiniMax.

title: Cal Water Finds No Evidence of OT Activity After Hackers Claimed They Could Disrupt Water Supply
blurb: Mandiant found no evidence of threat-actor activity in California Water Service's IT or OT environments, contradicting Iran-linked Handala's claim that it gained deep ICS access and could have disrupted the water supply. The investigation confirmed access was limited to a few user accounts on two third-party platforms and one customer account via stolen credentials, though Handala leaked 5 GB of data containing personal information.

title: GitLab Patches Code Execution, Information Disclosure Vulnerabilities
blurb: GitLab patched 13 vulnerabilities across CE and EE, led by CVE-2026-10712, an unauthenticated XSS in the Web IDE workbench asset handler that lets attackers execute JavaScript in victims' browser sessions. Two other high-severity bugs include an authenticated XSS in the Analytics dashboard (CVE-2026-10086) and information disclosure in Duo Workflows (CVE-2026-12053); fixes ship in versions 19.1.1, 19.0.3, and 18.11.6, and self-managed installs should upgrade immediately.

title: Stealthy new backdoor surfaces in attacks on multiple sectors
blurb: Symantec linked a stealthy new backdoor called Mistic to Woodgnat (aka KongTuke), a financially motivated initial access broker tied to Qilin, Interlock, Akira, and Black Basta ransomware operations targeting insurance, education, IT, and professional services since April 2026. Mistic side-loads through legitimate MpExtMs.exe using a DLL named EndpointDlp.dll to mimic Microsoft security tooling, executes code in memory, and includes a kill switch enabling durable, low-footprint access defenders should hunt for.

title: ‘This operation marked a shift in strategy’: Three notorious malware networks have been taken down using RICO legislation
blurb: Europol's Operation Endgame dismantled the infrastructure behind the SocGholish, Amadey, and StealC malware strains, neutralizing 326 servers and 142 domains and seizing over €41M in crypto with help from Microsoft and private security firms. US prosecutors applied RICO racketeering law to treat Amadey and StealC as a single conspiracy, a strategy shift toward disrupting the full malware-as-a-service chain rather than individual threats.

title: Europe Evolves Into Ransomware's Favorite Region
blurb: Black Kite tracked 684 ransomware attacks against European organizations in the first four months of 2026, a 55% jump from the 441 recorded over the same period in 2025 as RaaS groups pivot from a saturated US market. Researchers attribute the shift partly to AI-assisted target research that surfaces exposed EU targets, with defenders also absorbing downstream impact from ransomware hitting their vendors and suppliers.

title: Four arrested in Poland over crypto SIM-swap attacks; ZachXBT links ‘Merry’ to case
blurb: Polish authorities arrested four members of an organized criminal group accused of SIM-swap attacks on cryptocurrency exchanges, breaching telecom-adjacent IT infrastructure via social engineering to hijack victim phone numbers, drain exchange accounts, and launder tens of millions of zlotys, with FBI and HSI support. Onchain investigator ZachXBT alleges one detainee is Polish social-engineering actor Wojtek Kulisz, known as "Merry," matching designer items in raid footage to his Instagram.

title: Incode acquires Identiq, commits $100M to privacy-first identity tech
blurb: Incode Technologies acquired Identiq, an Israeli startup whose cryptographic tooling lets institutions confirm whether another organization has seen an identity before without exposing customer records, eliminating the shared fraud-signal databases that become breach targets. The deal anchors a $100M investment in privacy-preserving identity infrastructure, funding on-device biometric processing and privacy-enhancing research; terms were not disclosed.

title: Palo Alto Networks, IBM to match patching with AI pace for operational threats and more
blurb: Palo Alto Networks, IBM, and Red Hat expanded their alliance to pair Palo Alto's Virtual Patching with IBM and Red Hat's $5B Project Lightwell, deploying network-layer virtual patches the same day a vulnerability is found while open-source fixes are validated upstream. The venture targets the shrinking window between disclosure and exploit across open-source software, OT, commercial apps, and healthcare, with preemptive protection before official patches ship.

title: AI and Liability
blurb: A German court ruled that Google is liable for its AI search summaries, rejecting the defense that users know not to blindly trust AI-generated information and holding that the overviews are an expression of Google's own business activity. Schneier argues AI overviews rewrite third-party content with editorial discretion, placing them closer to a publisher than a carrier and outside the Section 230 shield that has long protected platforms.

title: New macOS malware embeds fake errors to confuse AI analysis tools
blurb: SentinelOne discovered macOS.Gaslight, a Rust backdoor and infostealer it attributes with high confidence to a North Korean-linked actor, which embeds a 3.5 KB cascade of 38 fake system-failure messages as prompt injection to make LLM-assisted triage agents doubt their session and abort analysis. The fabricated memory dumps, crash reports, and SQL injection alerts target the analysis agent's perception rather than the sandbox, so teams using AI-assisted reversing should isolate model output from execution decisions.

title: Russia allegedly used a forensics platform to hack an activist's phone, despite having its access cut off
blurb: Citizen Lab found that Russian authorities used Cellebrite's UFED Physical Analyzer and UFED 4PC toolkits to extract data from the iPhone 12 of jailed opposition activist Andrey Pivovarov around June 17, 2021, pulling WhatsApp, Telegram, and Viber content for his prosecution. Cellebrite says it had already cut off its Russian customers and that the hardware predates current sanctions, but the case shows seized devices stay extractable long after a vendor revokes access.

title: New MCP specification kills old risks but opens fresh attack surfaces, Akamai finds
blurb: Akamai's analysis of the MCP 2026-07-28 specification finds the overhaul removes protocol-level risks but shifts security burden onto developers. The rebuild kills protocol-managed sessions (eliminating Mcp-Session-Id hijacking), restricts server-initiated prompts, and mandates OAuth 2.1 with PKCE. The tradeoff: the new stateless model hands clients tracking IDs and state objects, so servers using predictable identifiers or skipping integrity validation expose workflow-hijacking and cross-tenant data access. Audit implementations before the July 28 release; the deprecation window for legacy functionality runs 12 months.

title: STOCKSTAY Another Day: The Latest Addition to Turla’s Intelligence Gathering Apparatus
blurb: Google's Threat Intelligence Group detailed STOCKSTAY, a .NET backdoor the Russia-linked espionage group Turla has developed and deployed since December 2022 against Ukrainian government and military targets and entities tied to Italian foreign policy. The backdoor shares substantial code with Turla's KAZUAR toolkit and uses encrypted WebSocket command-and-control, extending the FSB-attributed group's arsenal against Western foreign-affairs and defense organizations.

title: Chrome Ad Blocker with 10M+ Installs Found with Dormant Script Injection Capability
blurb: Island found that "Adblock for YouTube," a Featured Chrome extension with 10M+ installs, contains a dormant script-injection path that could run arbitrary JavaScript on any site through a single server-side config change, with no extension update or store review required. No malicious payload has shipped, but the capability lets IT teams treat Web Store badges and install counts as no guarantee of safety and tighten browser-extension allowlisting across managed fleets.

title: European Commission lines up Amazon and Microsoft for cloud gatekeeper status
blurb: The European Commission is preparing to designate Amazon and Microsoft as cloud gatekeepers under the Digital Markets Act, a status that would subject AWS and Azure to interoperability, data-portability, and anti-self-preferencing obligations. Enterprise buyers locked into either platform could gain mandated exit ramps and lower switching costs, while the designation pressures both vendors to unwind egress fees and licensing terms that the Commission views as lock-in.

title: Lantronix Serial-to-IP Converter Flaw Exploited in Attacks After OT Threat Warning
blurb: CISA added CVE-2025-67038, a flaw in Lantronix EDS5000 serial-to-IP device servers that lets unauthenticated attackers inject OS commands and execute them as root, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after confirming in-the-wild exploitation. Federal agencies have until June 26 to patch, and IT teams running the affected OT bridges should treat exposed units as a lateral-movement foothold given thousands remain internet-facing, mostly in the US.

title: Bluekit phishing kit adopts browser-in-the-middle for login theft
blurb: Bluekit, an AI-assisted phishing-as-a-service platform, switched to a browser-in-the-middle technique that uses the open-source rrweb library to stream a real login session to victims and capture valid session tokens, bypassing credential resets and MFA. Netcraft found nearly 70 new hostnames in the past week, and the session-token theft means IT teams should treat token revocation and conditional-access policies, not password rotation, as the primary containment control.

title: Apple says "the rapid expansion of AI data centers has created an extraordinary surge in demand for memory and storage"; the MacBook Neo rises from $599 to $699 (Mark Gurman/Bloomberg)
blurb: Apple warned that the rapid expansion of AI data centers has created an extraordinary surge in demand for memory and storage, and raised the entry-level MacBook Neo from $599 to $699 in response. The price hike signals that component cost inflation driven by hyperscaler buildouts is now reaching consumer hardware, a margin pressure enterprise buyers should expect across PC and server refresh cycles.

title: Apple Raises Prices on Some Products by $500
blurb: Apple raised prices on Macs, iPads, home devices, and the Vision Pro by up to $500 to offset a memory and storage chip shortage, while iPhone, Apple Watch, and AirPods pricing holds steady. IT buyers refreshing Mac fleets or deploying Vision Pro should expect higher hardware line items this cycle, with the chip shortage signaling broader device-cost pressure across vendors.

title: Cal Water Finds No Evidence of OT Activity After Hackers Claimed They Could Disrupt Water Supply
blurb: Cal Water found no evidence of threat-actor activity in its IT or operational technology environments after the Iran-linked group Handala claimed it could have disrupted the water supply, with Mandiant tracing the breach to unauthorized access of a few user accounts on two third-party platforms. The case underscores the gap between hacktivist disruption claims and verified ICS access in a water sector that remains a frequent target.

title: Salesforce unveils AI Help Agent with pay-per-resolution pricing
blurb: Salesforce introduced Agentforce Help Agent, a pre-packaged service agent that connects to a company's knowledge base to resolve customer and employee issues across chat, voice, and portal channels, billed only when it fully resolves a case for roughly $2 a pop. The pay-per-resolution model ties cost to outcomes rather than seats, pressuring rivals like Zendesk and Intercom whose per-agent pricing charges regardless of whether the bot deflects the ticket.

title: IBM stacks up a sub-nanometer chip future
blurb: IBM demonstrated a new chip process node it says can scale down to 1 Angstrom, pushing transistor density into sub-nanometer territory through 3D stacking. The roadmap signals continued density gains past the nanometer wall, which matters for enterprise buyers planning datacenter refresh cycles around the compute and power-efficiency curve that AI workloads now depend on.

title: Apple Hikes Mac, iPad Prices on Memory Shortage; Shares Fall
blurb: Apple raised prices across its Mac, iPad, home device, and Vision Pro lines on Thursday to offset an unprecedented memory-chip shortage driving up component costs. The increases signal that DRAM and NAND scarcity, fueled by AI infrastructure demand, is now hitting enterprise hardware budgets directly, and Apple shares fell as investors weighed the margin and demand risk.

title: Apple to Skip High-End M6 Mac Chips in Favor of AI-Focused M7 Line
blurb: Apple plans to skip the high-end M6 Mac chips and jump directly to an AI-focused M7 generation, launching M7 Pro, M7 Max, and M7 Ultra as its next top-end processors. The move signals Apple reorienting its Mac silicon roadmap around on-device AI workloads, a shift enterprise hardware buyers and Mac fleet managers will need to factor into refresh planning.

title: EU says AWS and Microsoft Azure should be treated as DMA gatekeepers
blurb: The European Commission informed Amazon and Microsoft of its preliminary view that AWS and Azure should be designated gatekeepers under the Digital Markets Act, citing entrenched user bases, high switching costs, and customer lock-in. If the finding is confirmed, both vendors get six months to comply with DMA rules on interoperability and data portability, which could ease egress costs and multi-cloud exits for European enterprise buyers.

title: Windows 10 quietly gets one more year of support and updates
blurb: Microsoft extended its consumer Extended Security Updates program for Windows 10 through October 12, 2027, pushing the security-only support deadline a full year past the previously announced 2026 cutoff. The quiet change spares enterprises and the hundreds of millions of devices still running Windows 10 from an immediate forced migration to Windows 11 or new hardware, easing near-term upgrade and procurement pressure.

title: IBM reveals sub-1nm chip technology, production expected in another 5 years
blurb: IBM announced the first sub-1nm chip technology, a 0.7nm "nanostack" architecture that vertically stacks nanosheet transistors to pack nearly 100 billion onto a fingernail-sized chip, with up to 50% higher performance or 70% better energy efficiency over its 2nm design. The milestone is research, not product. IBM exited manufacturing years ago, its 2021 2nm node still hasn't shipped at scale through partner Rapidus, and sub-1nm production depends on EUV tooling and years of yield work.

title: Windows 11 can now turn back the clock when updates go bad
blurb: Microsoft added a "quick machine recovery" rollback feature to Windows 11 that automatically reverts the OS to a working state when a botched update prevents the system from booting. The capability targets the unattended-update failures that strand IT helpdesks with manual recovery work, reducing field visits and downtime after Patch Tuesday rollouts go wrong on managed fleets.

title: Qualcomm acquires AI startup Modular in open ecosystem bet to challenge CUDA
blurb: Qualcomm acquired AI infrastructure startup Modular, the Chris Lattner-founded firm valued at $1.6B last year, to build a hardware-agnostic compute layer that challenges Nvidia's CUDA lock-in. The deal gives Qualcomm a "write once, run anywhere" software stack spanning GPU kernels to API endpoints as it pushes back into data center AI, betting enterprise buyers will pay for an open ecosystem that ports models across Nvidia, AMD, Arm, and Apple silicon.

title: ‘This operation marked a shift in strategy’: Three notorious malware networks have been taken down using RICO legislation
blurb: Europol's Operation Endgame dismantled the SocGholish, Amadey, and StealC malware networks in a joint action with Microsoft and security firms, taking down 326 servers and 142 domains, recovering 27 million stolen data sets, and seizing over €41M in crypto. The operation leaned on US RICO racketeering law to charge two malware families as a single conspiracy, signaling a shift toward disrupting the entire malware-as-a-service supply chain rather than individual threats.

title: RAM crisis bites Apple as unprecedented Mac and iPad price rises arrive — cheapest MacBook Pro price hiked by $400 to $1,999
blurb: Apple raised prices across its entire Mac and iPad lineup, with the entry-level MacBook Pro jumping $400 to $1,999 and the Mac Studio M4 Max climbing to $2,499 from $1,999, citing surging memory and storage component costs. The hikes signal that the RAM shortage is now overriding even Apple's buying power and vertical integration, a cost pressure enterprise hardware buyers should expect across vendors in upcoming refresh cycles.

title: New Linux Foundation project aims to bring DNS-style trust to AI agents
blurb: The Linux Foundation announced the Agent Name Service, a DNS-based framework that lets enterprises publish agent identities through domains they already control and verify an agent's owner, permissions, and code provenance before interacting with it. The federated design avoids any proprietary registry or centralized control, giving IT teams a standards-based way to close the agent identity and auditability gaps now surfacing in multi-agent, multi-vendor deployments.

title: Apple raises Mac, iPad, and other product prices by 15%-25%, saying it has "never seen a component price increase this much, this quickly"; iPhone is unchanged (Rolfe Winkler/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Apple raised prices on Macs, iPads, and other hardware by 15%-25%, citing component cost increases it called unprecedented in speed and scale, while leaving the iPhone unchanged. Enterprise buyers refreshing fleets of Macs and iPads face materially higher device budgets this cycle, and the iPhone carve-out suggests Apple is absorbing those costs only where competitive pressure is steepest.

title: OpenAI gets spicy with Broadcom via AI data movement-optimized ‘Jalapeño’ chip
blurb: OpenAI taped out its first custom AI chip, "Jalapeño," co-designed with Broadcom and optimized to cut data movement across compute, memory, and networking for inference workloads. The chip is the first step in a multi-generation roadmap that loosens OpenAI's dependence on Nvidia and positions Broadcom as its long-term silicon partner, reusing internal lines like ChatGPT and Codex to inform the design.

title: Apple passes RAMpocalypse costs on to consumers
blurb: Apple raised prices on iPads and Macs by hundreds of dollars, passing through the memory-chip cost spikes driven by AI datacenter demand soaking up DRAM supply. iPhone pricing is holding for now, but the increases signal that the RAM shortage is reaching enterprise hardware-refresh budgets, raising the TCO of standardized Mac and iPad fleets.

title: Amazon pours another $13B into India's AI and cloud infrastructure
blurb: Amazon committed another $13B to expand its India cloud and AI infrastructure, with new datacenter capacity in Mumbai and Hyderabad, as part of a broader $48B five-year investment pledge. The buildout deepens AWS's regional footprint against Microsoft Azure and Google Cloud, giving enterprises in India lower-latency, in-country options for data-residency-sensitive workloads.

title: Apple raises Mac and iPad prices, spares iPhone for now
blurb: Apple raised prices across its Mac and iPad lineups, blaming a memory and storage shortage driven by AI data-center buildout, while leaving the iPhone untouched for now. The MacBook Air jumped to $1,299 from $1,099 and the iPad Air to $749 from $599, a signal that DRAM and NAND inflation, up over fourfold since Q4 2025, is now flowing into enterprise hardware refresh budgets.

title: Apple ratchets up prices, blames the cost of memory
blurb: Apple raised prices across most of its Mac and iPad lineup, with increases ranging from $100 on entry-level models to $1,300 on the M3 Ultra Mac Studio, citing surging memory costs as AI demand pulls DRAM supply toward data centers.
